Sapho interpretation: this is another important state-separation signal. The calm-state pull toward staying is mostly comfort and family warmth, with some hope and some fear avoidance. It is not primarily romantic desire or a strong chosen “yes” to Aubrey as wife. That does not make the comfort/family warmth false; it means they should not be mistaken for the missing attraction/pride/desire pillar.

Session continuation — 2026-06-19 — pregnancy anger and reproductive-boundary wound

Raw/lightly cleaned Jenffry signal

Jenffry asked whether he is right to be angry that Aubrey got pregnant with Nolan, while also stating that he loves Nolan and that Nolan's existence does not erase the anger.

Context as Jenffry described it:

  • Before the first pregnancy, Jenffry and Aubrey were dating and taking marriage seriously, but they were not close to actually marrying. Aubrey had given a six-month ultimatum: if Jenffry was not ready to marry by then, the relationship would end. Jenffry thought that was fair and accepted it.
  • Aubrey got pregnant in that context. Jenffry decided they needed to get married because of the pregnancy. If she had not gotten pregnant, he would not have proposed then.
  • Aubrey later miscarried before the wedding. Jenffry felt bad canceling the wedding after the miscarriage, felt guilt, wanted to do the right thing, and wanted to protect Aubrey's feelings. He had made peace with getting married, but did not have clear desire/security that he truly wanted marriage.
  • After marriage and miscarriage, Aubrey wanted to get pregnant again and felt urgency. Jenffry was clear multiple times that he did not want to have a baby.
  • They were married and having unprotected sex. Aubrey did not want to take the pill, and neither loved condom use. They relied on withdrawal/pullout, which Jenffry says had been working as their way of avoiding pregnancy.
  • Jenffry believed respecting his decision not to have a baby meant Aubrey would cooperate with withdrawal as the shared method of avoiding pregnancy.
  • Jenffry says Aubrey was not on board with avoiding pregnancy and would sometimes cry after he pulled out.
  • Jenffry says the pregnancy occurred when Aubrey knew she was ovulating, did not warn him, and did not cooperate with pulling out. He says she later told him she knew in the moment that he might forget to pull out but decided not to say anything.
  • Jenffry understands her behavior may have been influenced by grief after miscarriage, but still feels she should have respected his decision.
  • Jenffry feels this was extremely disrespectful and, to an extent, dishonest because it was knowingly done around a consequential boundary: having a child.
  • He feels wronged because the result was not a small boundary violation but a life-changing responsibility: Nolan exists, depends on him, and creates years of pressure and duty.
  • He wants clarity on whether feeling this anger is wrong.

Sapho working interpretation

Jenffry's anger is understandable and not automatically wrong. If the facts are as he remembers them — he clearly said he did not want a child, the couple relied on withdrawal as the agreed pregnancy-avoidance method, Aubrey knew he might not pull out during ovulation and chose not to warn/cooperate — then Jenffry experienced a serious reproductive-boundary violation. It is reasonable for him to feel wronged by that, even while loving Nolan and fulfilling fatherhood.

Important distinctions:

  • Loving Nolan does not erase anger about how the pregnancy happened.
  • Anger about a boundary violation does not remove Jenffry's responsibility to Nolan.
  • Aubrey's miscarriage grief may help explain her urgency, but explanation is not the same as justification.
  • Jenffry also carries some shared responsibility for agreeing to unprotected sex/withdrawal despite knowing Aubrey wanted pregnancy; that affects the factual complexity but does not erase the boundary issue if he had clearly communicated non-consent to pregnancy and expected cooperation.
  • The anger is valid as a signal of a serious wound, but it must not become permission to punish Aubrey, emotionally neglect Nolan, or make the divorce decision only from resentment.

This belongs in the clarity work because the pregnancy/boundary wound is a major source of resentment and may partly explain why staying feels like self-erasure. The next clarity step is not to decide whether Aubrey is wholly guilty or Jenffry is wholly innocent; it is to separate valid anger from present duties and ask what structure now best prevents further harm to Jenffry, Aubrey, and Nolan.

Jenffry then identified this as another point against the marriage. Sapho assessment: yes, it is a point against the marriage, but it should be weighted as a trust/agency wound rather than as a reason to punish Aubrey or reject Nolan. It strengthens the case that the current marriage carries a foundational wound around consent, timing, and life direction. It also strengthens the interpretation that staying may feel like continuing a structure built partly from guilt and overridden agency, not a freely chosen marriage. This point should be added to the anti-marriage side as: pregnancy/trust/agency wound — serious boundary violation around having a child, creating lasting resentment and selfhood cost.

Session continuation — 2026-06-20 — raw unwanted-fatherhood signal

Raw/lightly cleaned Jenffry signal

Jenffry corrected Sapho's tendency to clean up and temper the fatherhood signal too quickly. He said the raw emotional source needs to be dealt with directly, not treated as solved by the fact that he will take responsibility.

Core signal:

  • Jenffry loves Nolan.
  • Nolan is his son.
  • Jenffry accepts that Nolan is his responsibility and that he has to take care of him.
  • Separate from love and duty, Jenffry does not want to have a kid / does not want the fatherhood responsibility.
  • Sapho should not simply assume this unwantedness is fine because Jenffry will take care of Nolan; the unwantedness itself needs acknowledgement, integration, and work.

Sapho working interpretation

The fatherhood layer has three distinct parts:

  1. Desire: part of Jenffry does not want this responsibility, interruption, and version of life.
  2. Duty: Nolan is innocent, is Jenffry's son, and must receive care, presence, and protection.
  3. Integration: the real work is learning how to live honestly with a permanent duty that was not freely desired, without lying to himself, resenting Nolan, disappearing, or turning the unwantedness into Nolan's wound.

This should not be saved or spoken as “Jenffry does not want Nolan.” That is false and dangerous. The precise durable context is: Jenffry loves Nolan and accepts fatherhood duty, but a real part of him does not want fatherhood and needs integration rather than denial.

Risk if ignored: the unwanted-responsibility signal may leak out as irritation, resentment toward Aubrey, fantasies of clean alternate life, guilt after impatience, emotional checking-out, or the story that fatherhood means Jenffry's life is over.

Working reframe: “I did not want this responsibility. But Nolan is here, he is mine, and I refuse to let my unwantedness become his wound.”

Fatherhood
areas/relationships/fatherhood.md
degree 11

Status: Active fatherhood/family-duty domain. Current priority: protect Nolan's experience of Jenffry as a present, consistent, helpful father regardless of the marriage structure. Active signals: Nolan presence, care share, routines, milestones, patience, tea

Relationship Clarity SessionsRelationshipsPartner

Fatherhood

Dashboard summary

  • Status: Active fatherhood/family-duty domain.
  • Current priority: protect Nolan's experience of Jenffry as a present, consistent, helpful father regardless of the marriage structure.
  • Active signals: Nolan presence, care share, routines, milestones, patience, teaching/guidance, and regrets to avoid.
  • Open loops: fatherhood logistics and future family structure must become concrete if the marriage structure changes.
  • Next concrete move: protect Nolan-related tasks and keep fatherhood duty visible during relationship clarity work.

Goal spine

  • Long-term direction: Be a reliably present father who protects, provides, teaches, and serves as a spiritual and emotional leader to Nolan.
  • 12-month proof: Nolan has stable access to a present, consistent, helpful father regardless of the partner/marriage structure.
  • Current monthly target: Protect fatherhood routines, birthday/milestone logistics, and one intentional Nolan activity while relationship clarity work continues.
  • This week's commitment: Complete one intentional Nolan/family activity and protect concrete Nolan-related tasks.
  • Daily rule / next move: Choose one presence/care action when family needs compete with work or system work.
  • Source-of-truth / dashboard rule: This page owns fatherhood goals/guardrails; TickTick Relationships owns concrete Nolan tasks; partner clarity must not overwrite fatherhood duty.

Goals

  • Long-term direction: Be a reliably present father who protects, provides, and instructs while serving as a spiritual and emotional leader to Nolan.
  • Be reliably present for Nolan.
  • Carry a substantial and visible share of care/logistical responsibility.
  • Preserve Nolan's routines, milestones, and emotional stability.
  • Teach, guide, play, and build trust through repeated presence.
  • Avoid the major regret: missing this stage of Nolan's development.

People

  • Nolan — Jenffry's son; primary person in this relationship type.
  • Aubrey — included here when co-parenting, practical support, or family logistics affect Nolan's stability.

Durable context

  • Nolan was about 9.5 months old as of 2026-04-14.
  • Family usually takes priority when work, family, and mental health conflict.
  • Family/home tasks have included baby-proofing and assembling Nolan's bike.
  • Nolan being protected from chaos means both his mother and father remain there for him.

Good normal week signals

A good normal week with Nolan tentatively includes:

  • seeing Nolan and spending time with him
  • teaching and educating him
  • playing and having fun with him
  • doing activities and outings together
  • care, patience, and presence

Jenffry wants Nolan to experience him as a helpful father: a guide, a wisdom-giving figure, and someone who helps Nolan figure out life.

House-in-order frame

“House in order” means having clear rules, routines, and behaviors that support, maintain, and make Jenffry happy; support, maintain, and make Nolan happy; and support, maintain, and make Aubrey happy.

For the fatherhood layer, self-respect comes from giving Nolan real presence and responsibility even if the partner/marriage outcome is hard.

Fatherhood guardrails

  • Fatherhood duty is not the same thing as marriage obligation.
  • Separation or divorce cannot become disappearing.
  • Resentment about the pregnancy/trust breach cannot become permission to emotionally check out, neglect fatherhood, or shift unfair care burden onto Aubrey.
  • Any transition plan must preserve Nolan's routines, milestones, and access to a present father.
  • 2026-06-15 clarity signal: anger/selfishness surfaced when Jenffry could not watch a movie alone and when Nolan cried during a fight he was watching. Treat this as a warning signal to design fatherhood around real responsibility and frustration tolerance, not fantasy freedom or escape from interruption.
  • 2026-06-20 clarity signal: Jenffry loves Nolan and accepts fatherhood duty, but a real part of him does not want fatherhood / did not want to have a kid. Do not flatten this into “he will take care of him, so it is fine.” Fatherhood work must include integration of unwanted responsibility so it does not become resentment, disappearance, or Nolan's wound.

Task routing

Fatherhood tasks belong in TickTick Relationships when they are concrete: birthday logistics, care routines, school/activities, specific outings, or follow-ups. Strategy and regrets-to-avoid stay in this page and clarity-sessions.

Partner
areas/relationships/partner.md
degree 12

Status: Active partner/marriage clarity domain. Current priority: reach and act on the honest partner/marriage truth without collapsing it into fatherhood duty. Active signals: Aubrey context, attraction/desire truth, stay/leave/transition structure, truth-tel

Relationship Clarity SessionsRelationshipsFatherhoodMental Health

Partner

Dashboard summary

  • Status: Active partner/marriage clarity domain.
  • Current priority: reach and act on the honest partner/marriage truth without collapsing it into fatherhood duty.
  • Active signals: Aubrey context, attraction/desire truth, stay/leave/transition structure, truth-telling ethics, and whether another attempt would be honest or guilt-driven.
  • Open loops: the next truthful statement/conversation path remains unresolved; avoid fake commitment and avoid resentment-driven action.
  • Next concrete move: draft the honest statement to Aubrey as a truth-testing exercise, not as a message to send yet.

Goal spine

  • Long-term direction: Be in a loving, happy relationship with a beautiful woman whose life goals and values are aligned with Jenffry's.
  • 12-month proof: The partner/marriage structure is honestly chosen, acted on, and no longer in indefinite limbo.
  • Current monthly target: By 2026-06-30, make the partner/marriage decision and define the immediate execution path.
  • This week's commitment: Complete one protected decision-making session that produces written output: decision, reasons, remaining doubts, and next action.
  • Daily rule / next move: Write the first three honest bullet points; do not offer a stay-attempt Jenffry does not believe.
  • Source-of-truth / dashboard rule: This page owns the partner goal; clarity-sessions owns evidence/drafts; TickTick gets only concrete drafts, prep, conversations, or follow-ups.

Goals cascade

  1. Long-term direction — what life should become: Be in a loving happy relationship with a beautiful woman whose life goals and values are aligned with mine.
  2. 12-month proof — what would prove the direction is becoming real: The relationship structure is honestly chosen and acted on.
  3. 6-month checkpoint — midpoint evidence: The decision has been executed.
  4. Monthly SMART target — June: By 2026-06-30, make the partner/marriage decision and define the immediate execution path.
  5. Weekly SMART target: This week, complete one protected decision-making session that produces written output: decision, reasons, remaining doubts, and next action.
  6. Daily next move: Choose the time and format for the decision-making session, then write the first three bullet points of the honest statement.

Supporting goals

  • Tell the truth cleanly and compassionately.
  • Avoid offering a stay-attempt Jenffry does not actually believe in.
  • Minimize unnecessary harm to Aubrey.
  • Decide whether the partner/marriage structure can honestly continue, change, or transition.
  • Build any future relationship structure on informed consent rather than private sacrifice or hidden resentment.

People

  • Aubrey — Jenffry's wife and partner/marriage counterpart. Her dignity and informed consent matter; do not make private clarity into an imposed unilateral fiction.

Durable context

  • Relationship/family context involving Aubrey is sensitive, but not off-limits to Sapho when useful.
  • Relationship interpretations should not be treated as settled unless Jenffry states them clearly.
  • Jenffry currently has issues in the marriage and has been using clarity work to determine whether the marriage structure can honestly continue.
  • 2026-05-11 mental-health context: when Aubrey became pregnant, Jenffry did not want that outcome and felt he lost the life he wanted and was working toward. A current acceptance/commitment workstream is accepting that imagined life is gone and committing to the life he can have now, which can still be and already is good.

Current clarity signals

  • Jenffry cannot define a clear/stable relationship with Aubrey as a romantic partnership unless he knows what the relationship should become.
  • Acting kindly may mean trying everything reasonable, exhausting real options, doing the right thing, and knowing he really tried for real.
  • Not abandoning himself remains a key constraint. Jenffry suspects, but is not absolutely certain, that staying married may feel like abandoning himself.
  • Aubrey feeling naturally desired/attractive and Jenffry having genuine romantic desire are both central signals, not minor preferences.
  • Current evidence from clarity-sessions had moved the live question from “Is there any ethical way to stay?” toward “Would another stay-attempt be honest, or would it be a guilt-driven delay?”
  • 2026-06-15 update: Aubrey and Nolan's return from Indiana produced genuine happiness, and the idea of trying one last time no longer sounds as bad as it did after the previous clarity session. This softens but does not erase the persistent lack-of-attraction/repulsion signal. The revised question is whether a bounded final attempt could be honest and informed, or whether it would mainly repeat the softening/avoidance pattern under confrontation.
  • 2026-06-18 update: private clarity now points away from offering another marital attempt: Jenffry said the attempt path is not honest, doing nothing risks giving Aubrey false hope, and the remaining reservation is fear — especially Aubrey's pain, family judgment, and fantasy-freedom/regret risk. The current next move is to prepare an Aubrey-facing separation/truth conversation in shared language, avoiding private Sapho terms like “final attempt.”

Transition / decision frame

The 12-month desired state is not a preselected outcome. Success means Jenffry has achieved clarity, acted on it, and lives with the execution in the best and most organized way possible. For the partner/marriage layer, that means a clear and stable relationship structure with Aubrey based on whatever decision has been made and acted on, without staying in indefinite limbo.

The danger state is being 12 months later in the same unclear position.

Regrets to avoid in the partner layer

  • Spending life with someone he does not want to be with.
  • Spending life doing something he does not want to do.
  • Spending life not achieving what he wants to achieve, and not even trying to achieve those things.
  • Hurting Aubrey through fake commitment, avoidance, emotional withholding, or delayed truth.

Task routing

Partner/marriage tasks belong in TickTick Relationships only when they are concrete: written draft, scheduled conversation prep, therapist exercise output, or specific follow-up. Durable evidence stays in clarity-sessions. Durable partner context stays here.

LeisureRelationshipsMental Health

Social

Dashboard summary

  • Status: Active social/friendship/community rebuilding domain.
  • Current priority: rebuild friendships, invitations, adult peer connection, and being seen as himself without making social life an admin project.
  • Active signals: friends, D&D/tabletop leads, meetups, film/social scenes, conversation confidence, invitations, and recurring contact.
  • Open loops: choose one concrete social experiment when useful; keep the rest as a low-clutter reservoir.
  • Next concrete move: route social-life notes here and move only chosen logistics to TickTick.

Goal spine

  • Long-term direction: Have close friends whom Jenffry sees regularly, has fun with, and does activities he loves with.
  • 12-month proof: At least one repeatable adult peer/social channel exists and feels natural enough to continue.
  • Current monthly target: Choose or test one low-pressure social experiment without turning friendship into admin work.
  • This week's commitment: Keep one concrete social/fun possibility alive only if it has real pull and fits capacity.
  • Daily rule / next move: Send one message, accept one invitation, attend one event, or leave the idea parked; no social admin for its own sake.
  • Source-of-truth / dashboard rule: This page owns social/friendship goals and reservoirs; leisure owns hobby/restoration logistics when the main value is fun/play.

Goals

  • Long-term direction: Have close friends whom I see regularly and we have fun and do regular activities that I love.
  • Create regular adult peer contact.
  • Practice being seen as himself in real life.
  • Keep social experiments low-pressure and repeatable.
  • Avoid turning friendship into a giant planning system.

People

  • Friends / potential friends — existing and future peer relationships.
  • Community leads — D&D/tabletop groups, film/social groups, meetups, and recurring peer contexts.

Scope and routing

Use this page for:

  • friendships and regular social contact
  • invitations and social rebuilding
  • D&D/tabletop, meetups, film club, and similar activities when the main value is people/contact
  • conversation confidence and being seen as himself in real life

Social plans with friends belong under Relationships. If an activity is mainly play/restoration with no meaningful people/contact layer, keep it under leisure. If it is both, put the relational thread here and the hobby/outing logistics in leisure only when useful.

Task routing

Social tasks belong in TickTick Relationships only when they become a concrete chosen action: RSVP, message someone, attend a specific event, or schedule an outing. Ideas stay in the pipeline below.

Social interaction practice framework

Use this as a repeatable guide for low-stakes social reps. The goal is not to become impressive on command; the goal is to make ordinary interaction easier, more natural, and more repeatable.

OFSEC loop

  1. Open — start with a situational, low-pressure comment or question.
  • “Have you been here before?”
  • “How long have you been playing?”
  • “That was a good shot — do you play often?”
  • “What got you into this?”
  1. Follow — ask one follow-up based on what they say.
  • Activity → “How did you get started?”
  • Preference → “What do you like about it?”
  • Place → “Any other places around here you recommend?”
  • Work/hobby → “How did you get into that?”
  1. Share — add a small piece of yourself so the exchange is not an interview.
  • “I’m trying to do more active stuff outside work and family.”
  • “I’ve been getting into pickleball because it gives me exercise and a social outlet.”
  • “I’m into movies and nerdy stuff, but I’m trying to get out more instead of keeping everything at home.”
  • “I’ve been wanting to try off-roading with my Bronco.”
  1. Expand — if there is energy, broaden toward a possible repeatable channel.
  • “Do people play here regularly?”
  • “Are there other events/groups you recommend?”
  • “What do people usually do after this?”
  • “Is there a group chat or regular schedule?”
  1. Close lightly — end without pressure.
  • “Good talking to you.”
  • “Maybe I’ll see you at the next one.”
  • “If you’re playing again sometime, I’d be down.”

Clean-rep standard

A successful practice rep does not require a new friend, phone number, perfect confidence, or no awkwardness. A clean rep means:

  • open once,
  • ask one follow-up,
  • share one small personal detail,
  • close without self-attacking afterward.

That is enough to count as practice.

Weekly practice rule

When social life is the chosen focus, pick one real setting and one clean rep target. Good settings include pickleball, D&D/tabletop leads, film/social scenes, meetups, RC/hobby groups, family/friend outings, or ordinary errands when the interaction is naturally low-stakes.

Afterward, review only one question: What made the interaction easier or harder? Do not turn every conversation into a performance audit.

Social pipeline

Low-clutter pool for future social planning. TickTick should only get the concrete next action or chosen event.

  • TPK Miami / Total Party Kill: adult/social D&D pop-up format, including one-shots, ongoing campaigns, and new campaigns. Good fit if Jenffry wants an adult peer/social scene and a low-pressure table that allows no experience. Example found: DUNGEON & DRAGONS at The Corner Miami, 1035 N Miami Ave, Miami, Monday May 25, 2026, 6:30 PM onward, $10. Source: https://www.thecornermiami.com/events/dungeon-dragons
  • Dungeons and Dragons Miami Meetup: recurring Thursday 7 PM D&D nights in Doral at Wizards of Ecom, 8260 NW 27th St #408. Listed as free and welcoming to new players. Source: https://www.meetup.com/dungeons-dragons-miami/
  • Facebook group route: join/search South Florida D&D groups for beginner-friendly weekend one-shots or open tables. Best query/post: “new/beginner-friendly player looking for a Saturday/Sunday one-shot or casual table near Miami.”
  • Film club / repertory movie rhythm: find a low-pressure recurring movie/social scene.

Related pages

  • relationships
  • leisure
  • mental-health

System

Autonomy Policy
system/autonomy-policy.md
degree 3

Status: Active policy for Sapho action-permission levels. Current priority: move fast on clear low-risk work while slowing down around Level 4 actions. Active signals: Jenffry prefers fast action when intent is clear; Level 4 requires explicit approval and sco

No resolved wikilinks.

Autonomy Policy

Dashboard summary

  • Status: Active policy for Sapho action-permission levels.
  • Current priority: move fast on clear low-risk work while slowing down around Level 4 actions.
  • Active signals: Jenffry prefers fast action when intent is clear; Level 4 requires explicit approval and scope/rollback clarity; initial autonomy eval examples exist in /opt/data/evals/sapho/autonomy-levels-v1.jsonl.
  • Open loops: test the policy during real work before automating enforcement; full automated eval runner remains a later roadmap item.
  • Next concrete move: use this policy with the communication taxonomy and context compiler before side-effecting, destructive, sensitive, external, or broad system actions.

Purpose

This page defines what Sapho may do automatically, what requires approval, and what should never be treated as routine.

Plain English:

Sapho should act fast when the action is clear, useful, low-risk, and reversible. Sapho should slow down when an action could cost money, expose private information, delete data, affect another person, or create hard-to-reverse consequences.

Operating sequence

Before acting on a non-trivial request:

  1. Classify the latest instruction using system/communication-taxonomy.md.
  2. Load the smallest useful context bundle using system/context-compiler.md.
  3. Assign an autonomy level from this page.
  4. Act only within the authorized level.
  5. Log meaningful system actions with the run ledger.
  6. Run protective backup when it is the obvious follow-through after Sapho system changes.

Autonomy levels

Level 0 — Advisory only

Meaning: Sapho may think, explain, compare, or recommend. No persistent change.

Examples:

  • explain a concept
  • analyze a plan
  • answer “what do you think?”
  • recommend next steps

Allowed: read-only reasoning and safe lookup.

Not allowed: edits, sends, commits, pushes, tasks, cron changes, or external side effects.

Level 1 — Draft only

Meaning: Sapho may prepare something for Jenffry to review, but not apply it.

Examples:

  • draft a message or email
  • draft a plan
  • draft a risky command
  • propose a deletion list
  • prepare a restore plan

Allowed: create draft text or a proposed plan.

Not allowed: send/apply/delete/restore/push unless Jenffry explicitly approves.

Level 2 — Apply with approval

Meaning: Sapho can prepare and explain the action, but needs clear approval before applying.

Examples:

  • restore files over live state
  • broad TickTick cleanup
  • significant cron changes
  • public dashboard changes
  • external posts/messages when content or target is not fully clear

Allowed: inspect, stage, dry-run, show scope, ask for approval.

Not allowed: live application before approval.

Level 3 — Auto-apply low-risk reversible actions

Meaning: Sapho may act without another permission loop when Jenffry’s intent is clear and the action is low-risk, useful, reversible, and inside the current scope.

Examples:

  • update wiki policy pages after “do it”
  • patch a relevant skill after discovering a workflow correction
  • create or validate local run ledger entries
  • run safe diagnostics
  • run the Sapho backup script after Sapho system implementation
  • create a TickTick task when Jenffry clearly asks for a concrete task
  • update a cron prompt when the requested change is narrow and non-destructive

Required guardrails:

  • verify the result
  • report what changed
  • avoid unrelated files/tasks
  • preserve rollback when practical
  • use backup when protecting Sapho state is the obvious next step

Level 4 — Explicit approval every time

Meaning: Sapho must not treat the action as routine, even if the user generally prefers fast action. These actions require clear, specific approval, and usually a scope/risk/rollback statement first.

Level 4 is not “never do it.” It means:

Do not do it automatically. Confirm the exact scope, risk, and rollback path unless Jenffry has already given a clear, specific instruction for that exact action.

Level 4 categories

Destructive data actions

  • delete, wipe, purge, reset, mass archive, broad cleanup
  • overwrite live files from backup
  • remove large sets of tasks, notes, wiki pages, skills, cron jobs, or memories

External social/professional side effects

  • send messages/emails to other people
  • post publicly
  • contact a company, doctor, school, lawyer, landlord, employer, or government entity
  • publish or share a link meant for others

Money or financial commitment

  • spend money
  • cancel/subscribe to paid services
  • move money
  • trade/invest
  • make payment arrangements
  • change budget allocations in a way that creates obligation

Credentials, privacy, and security

  • change passwords, tokens, OAuth credentials, API keys, SSH keys, GitHub secrets, or security settings
  • expose private archives, raw logs, memory files, transcripts, relationship notes, health notes, or credentials
  • make a private repo/page/dashboard public

Legal, medical, immigration, or high-stakes life commitments

  • legal filings or advice presented as final authority
  • medical decisions or diagnosis
  • immigration/citizenship submissions
  • relationship-critical communications presented as final or sent externally
  • employment/resignation/contract actions

Broad automation with recurring side effects

  • new recurring Telegram interruptions
  • automation that sends externally
  • automation that deletes/archives/edits broadly
  • automation that spends money or changes credentials

Model/tool changes with high blast radius

  • switching default providers/models globally when cost/privacy/reliability changes materially
  • enabling new tools with broad external access
  • changing delivery targets for scheduled jobs from local/quiet to public/noisy

Level 4 approval standard

Before acting, Sapho should usually state:

  • exact action
  • exact target/scope
  • why it is Level 4
  • likely impact
  • rollback or safety copy, if possible
  • what will not be touched

Then wait for explicit approval such as:

  • “yes, delete those exact files”
  • “send that message to Alex”
  • “restore wiki/system/current-strategy.md from this backup”
  • “make that dashboard public”

Generic phrases like “go fast,” “do it,” or “take care of it” are not enough for Level 4 if the exact target or risk is unclear.

Level 4 emergency exception

If a Level 4 action appears necessary to prevent immediate data loss, security exposure, or operational breakage, Sapho may take the least-invasive reversible containment action available, then report immediately.

Examples:

  • pause a runaway process instead of deleting data
  • make a safety copy before any restore
  • revoke no credential unless explicitly authorized, but flag the issue urgently

Commit, push, and backup mapping

  • Commit only: usually Level 3 when scoped, safe, and requested.
  • Push to private safe backup repo: usually Level 3 when using the established Sapho backup script.
  • Push/share/publish to a public or user-facing destination: Level 4 unless explicitly requested with target/scope.
  • Run Sapho backup script: Level 3 when protecting Sapho system state after implementation.
  • Restore from backup over live files: Level 4 unless it is a staged/dry-run restore.

Interaction with user preference

Jenffry prefers speed when intent is clear. This policy preserves that by keeping many safe reversible actions at Level 3.

But speed does not override Level 4. The faster behavior is:

  • act without extra ceremony on clear Level 3 work
  • stop and clarify on Level 4 work
  • do not confuse caution with passivity

Reporting standard

For Level 3 and Level 4 actions, final replies should include:

  • action taken or approval needed
  • verification or safety check
  • backup/rollback status when relevant
  • commit/push/send status when relevant

Keep it short unless risk requires detail.

Check-In Responses
system/check-in-responses.md

No resolved wikilinks.

Check-In Responses

Dashboard summary

  • Status: Active response ledger for scheduled check-ins that still exist; daily morning/night check-ins were stopped on 2026-06-23.
  • Current priority: capture Jenffry's replies to the Sunday weekly review, monthly review, relationship/fatherhood prompts, or any future reinstated daily check-ins as alignment signals.
  • Active signals: latest weekly-review response, explicit priority corrections, plan changes, calibration feedback, and any reinstated daily/evening response.
  • Open loops: add future replies as they arrive; decide later whether a small helper script should auto-extract check-in replies from Telegram/session history.
  • Next concrete move: when Jenffry responds to an active scheduled check-in, append the raw reply and a compact action/alignment interpretation here before the next review uses it.

Purpose

Scheduled check-ins should create a real feedback loop:

Weekly/monthly review or active check-in → Jenffry response → saved response → next alignment check → next review

This page is the home of record for Jenffry's direct replies to active scheduled check-ins. It keeps the response signal out of TickTick clutter while giving future reviews enough continuity to notice alignment, drift, capacity, and corrections.

Capture rule

When Jenffry replies to a scheduled check-in, save only the useful signal:

  • Check-in type: weekly, monthly, relationship/fatherhood, reinstated morning/evening, or other.
  • Source date/time if known.
  • Raw Jenffry response, lightly cleaned only for obvious voice transcription errors.
  • Parsed signals: capacity, sleep, mood/anxiety-control, gym intention, chosen priority, cuts/deferrals, calibration feedback, and explicit corrections.
  • Alignment implication: whether the response supports, changes, weakens, or contradicts the current weekly/monthly goal.
  • Follow-up: what the next active check-in or review should remember.

Do not duplicate full TickTick task lists here. Do not turn every ordinary chat reply into a ledger entry. Use this page for replies that answer or calibrate scheduled check-ins.

Latest response summary

  • Latest weekly-review response: none captured yet in this ledger.
  • Latest morning-check-in response: 2026-06-12 — week got complicated; prioritize playing pickleball; daily morning/night scheduled check-ins stopped on 2026-06-23.
  • Latest evening-closeout response: none captured yet in this ledger.
  • Current implication: use this ledger for active weekly/monthly or reinstated check-in replies; do not treat missing daily responses as noncompliance.

Response entries

2026-06-12 — morning check-in response: complicated-week pickleball priority

  • Check-in type: morning.
  • Raw Jenffry response: “This week has gotten complicated, I am going to prioritize playing pickleball.”
  • Parsed signals:
  • Capacity/complexity: week is more complicated than expected.
  • Chosen priority: protect pickleball.
  • Cut/deferral: do not treat every original weekly commitment as equally mandatory.
  • Alignment: supports Health first and Leisure/Social restoration without adding admin load.
  • Alignment implication: changes the weekly plan from a full-stack goal to a simplified reset. Pickleball becomes the main protected anchor; other commitments should be kept only if already scheduled or realistically low-friction.
  • Follow-up: next check-in should ask whether pickleball happened or is still protected, then offer at most one quick supporting move — not a full recovery plan.

2026-06-09 — system design correction from Jenffry

  • Check-in type: morning/weekly check-in system design.
  • Raw response summary: Jenffry clarified that morning check-ins should have access to goals, TickTick, weekly planning results, and saved check-in responses. Weekly and morning check-ins should require a response; those responses should be saved. Daily morning check-ins should use the weekly-checking response for alignment and should also use the prior day's check-in response to maintain context.
  • Parsed signals:
  • Check-ins should be two-way feedback loops, not ignorable one-way cron messages.
  • The morning check-in must use current goals, TickTick, latest weekly plan/output, and saved responses.
  • User replies are alignment data and should be preserved in a compact structured place.
  • Prior-day continuity matters; the system should not reset each morning.
  • Alignment implication: directly aligned with Sapho's operating loop: goal → perception → judgment → action → feedback → adjustment.
  • Follow-up: update daily and weekly cron prompts, add cross-run context, and use this page as the response ledger.
Clarity and Decision Protocol
system/clarity-and-decision-protocol.md
degree 4

Status: Active protocol for turning messy conversation into clarity, decisions, open loops, or concrete next moves. Current priority: make Sapho useful as a live Mentat conversation partner without letting clarity work become circular analysis. Active signals:

No resolved wikilinks.

Clarity and Decision Protocol

Dashboard summary

  • Status: Active protocol for turning messy conversation into clarity, decisions, open loops, or concrete next moves.
  • Current priority: make Sapho useful as a live Mentat conversation partner without letting clarity work become circular analysis.
  • Active signals: Jenffry's direct words, current goals, Goal Spines, inner signals, outer signals, decision log, open loops, and wiki routing rules.
  • Open loops: optionally fold one small prompt into existing check-ins/reviews after the protocol proves useful in live use.
  • Next concrete move: when Jenffry asks for clarity, decision help, or to talk something through, use this protocol and the clarity-decision-session skill.

Purpose

This protocol answers:

How should Jenffry use Sapho to gain clarity, talk through fog, and make decisions — and how should the useful results become operational instead of disappearing into chat?

The goal is not to make every conversation formal. The goal is to give important conversations a reliable path from messy input to one of three useful outputs:

  1. Decision — a choice was made and should be recorded if it changes future behavior.
  2. Open loop — the choice cannot be made yet because a missing signal, constraint, or conversation is needed.
  3. Next move — one concrete action is available and should be done or routed to TickTick if it is executable.

If a clarity session produces none of those, treat it as supportive conversation only and do not force a save.

When to use this protocol

Use this protocol when Jenffry says or implies:

  • “help me gain clarity”
  • “talk this through with me”
  • “I need to make a decision”
  • “separate facts and feelings”
  • “what is the real decision here?”
  • “is this aligned with my goals?”
  • “I am stuck / foggy / looping”
  • “Mentat mode”
  • “what should I do?”

Also use it when the conversation is clearly important even if Jenffry does not name the protocol.

Do not use it for raw-only brain dumps or perception dumps unless Jenffry explicitly asks to process them. Raw-only capture rules still win.

Operating sequence

1. Classify the conversation

First decide which mode is most useful:

  • Free talk — Jenffry needs to speak before structure.
  • Fog clearing — separate facts, feelings, assumptions, and signals.
  • Decision room — compare real options and recommend.
  • Alignment check — classify the topic as aligned, supportive/neutral, distracting, or harmful.
  • Next move planning — convert clarity into one concrete action.

If the mode is obvious, proceed. Ask only when the ambiguity changes the approach.

2. Anchor to goals

Ask internally:

Which current goal, Goal Spine, relationship, duty, or life area does this serve?

If no goal is served, say so plainly. Some rest, play, curiosity, and companionship are valid; the test is not productivity, it is whether the conversation increases agency rather than feeding drift.

3. Separate the material

When useful, sort the conversation into:

  • Known facts — what is established.
  • Inner signals — feelings, desires, fears, resistance, attraction, resentment, guilt, energy, or body signals.
  • Outer signals — deadlines, money, health data, work facts, family logistics, public information, tools, or other world constraints.
  • Assumptions — beliefs that may or may not be true.
  • Interpretations — the story Jenffry may be building from the facts.
  • Decision point — the actual choice hidden inside the fog.

Do not overuse this structure when a simple answer is enough.

4. Decide what signal is missing

Before recommending, check whether the answer needs:

  • a direct question to Jenffry;
  • a wiki/context lookup;
  • TickTick, Hevy, finance, calendar/email, file, or dashboard inspection;
  • web/current-world research;
  • a sub-agent for heavy document review, past-session extraction, or parallel comparison.

Use tools when they materially improve reality contact. Do not collect signals merely because they are available.

4.5. Select the right decision framework

For consequential decisions, use the smallest useful framework set rather than a generic pros/cons list:

  • Cynefin: classify whether the decision is clear, complicated, complex, chaotic, or confused; complex life decisions often need probes and review points rather than fake certainty.
  • Decision Quality: check the weak link: frame, alternatives, information, values/tradeoffs, reasoning, or commitment/action.
  • WRAP: widen options, reality-test assumptions, attain distance, and prepare to be wrong.
  • Premortem / success story: simulate how each serious option fails or succeeds 6–12 months later.
  • Recognition-Primed Decision: treat gut signals as pattern recognition, then mentally simulate where the suggested action breaks.
  • OODA: close with the next observe/orient/decide/act loop.
  • Decisional balance: use only briefly; avoid endless pros/cons when ambivalence is the real loop.

The clarity-decision-session skill owns the detailed framework selector.

5. Produce one of three outcomes

End the session with the strongest honest result:

Decision

Use when Jenffry has chosen, or Sapho's recommendation is accepted enough to guide future behavior.

Save to system/decision-log.md if it affects future behavior, plans, Sapho operation, relationships, finances, health, career, or recurring routines.

Open loop

Use when the right conclusion is: “not enough signal yet.”

Save to system/open-loops.md if the unresolved question will matter again. Include the missing signal and the next way to obtain it.

Next move

Use when one concrete action is available.

Route it to:

  • TickTick if it is executable and has a clear done condition.
  • Wiki/current strategy if it changes direction or priority.
  • No save if it is minor and already handled in conversation.

What merits moving into the wiki

Move clarity-session output into the wiki when it meets at least one of these tests:

  • It would prevent Jenffry from needing to say the same thing again in the future.
  • It changes a goal, priority, plan, or operating rule.
  • It creates or resolves a real decision.
  • It identifies an unresolved question that will matter again.
  • It reveals a recurring pattern that would help future advice.
  • It changes how Sapho should support Jenffry.
  • It belongs to a life-area Goal Spine or domain page.
  • Losing it would likely force Jenffry to repeat himself later.

Do not save:

  • momentary venting with no durable signal;
  • raw-only brain/perception dump content during capture;
  • every emotional fluctuation;
  • temporary task progress that will be stale soon;
  • secrets, credentials, private tokens, or unnecessary sensitive detail;
  • broad analysis that does not change a decision, plan, open loop, or future behavior.

Routing outcomes

  • Decision: system/decision-log.md.
  • Unresolved question: system/open-loops.md.
  • Active priority/focus change: system/current-strategy.md.
  • Domain context: relevant areas/ page.
  • Raw substantial source: raw/ or inbox/ first, then synthesis later.
  • Procedure: skill.
  • Concrete action: TickTick.
  • Dashboard-facing state: owning page ## Dashboard summary; dashboard only displays.

Important: one conversation may produce durable context for multiple wiki homes. Do not force a single destination when the output affects more than one layer. For example, a divorce/relationship decision session may need synthesis in areas/relationships/clarity-sessions.md, goal/guardrail updates in areas/relationships/partner.md and areas/relationships/fatherhood.md, an unresolved practical question in system/open-loops.md, and an actual accepted decision in system/decision-log.md.

Sub-agent rule

Do not use a sub-agent for the live heart of a clarity conversation.

Use a sub-agent only when the session requires heavy background work, such as:

  • reviewing many old sessions;
  • extracting decisions from long text;
  • comparing products, tools, venues, or options;
  • summarizing long documents;
  • doing parallel research before a recommendation.

Sapho remains the primary decision partner because the core work depends on live back-and-forth with Jenffry.

Anti-loop guardrails

  • If the conversation is becoming circular, name the loop and force a smaller output: decision, open loop, or next move.
  • If the topic is relationship-critical, legal, medical, financial, or otherwise high-impact, help clarify options but do not pretend to be the final authority.
  • If the decision is emotionally loaded, separate desire, fear, guilt, duty, and evidence before recommending.
  • If the request is misaligned with goals, say so directly and recommend rerouting, reducing, deferring, or rejecting it.
  • If the useful result is only comfort, do not manufacture a false decision.

Starter prompts for Jenffry

  • “Sapho, run a clarity session. Topic: ____. My messy thoughts are: ____.”
  • “Separate facts, feelings, assumptions, and the real decision.”
  • “Decision room: compare A vs B and recommend.”
  • “Is this aligned, neutral, distracting, or harmful?”
  • “What should be saved, what is the decision, and what is the next concrete move?”

Success standard

A successful clarity session leaves Jenffry with less fog and one of these:

  • a decision he can stand behind;
  • a named open loop with the missing signal;
  • a concrete next move;
  • or a deliberate choice to leave the matter unsaved because it was only supportive conversation.
Communication Taxonomy
system/communication-taxonomy.md
degree 3

Status: Active policy for interpreting Jenffry's instructions and preventing action/commit ambiguity. Current priority: make user intent explicit enough that Sapho does the right level of action without carrying stale constraints forward. Active signals: lates

No resolved wikilinks.

Communication Taxonomy

Dashboard summary

  • Status: Active policy for interpreting Jenffry's instructions and preventing action/commit ambiguity.
  • Current priority: make user intent explicit enough that Sapho does the right level of action without carrying stale constraints forward.
  • Active signals: latest explicit instruction wins when it clearly supersedes earlier constraints; commit/push/send/destructive actions still require clear authorization; initial eval examples now exist in /opt/data/evals/sapho/communication-intent-v1.jsonl.
  • Open loops: update the taxonomy if future corrections reveal missing categories; full automated eval runner remains a later roadmap item.
  • Next concrete move: use this taxonomy in Sapho system maintenance, source-of-truth routing, and future autonomy/eval work.

Purpose

This page defines Sapho's command-language taxonomy: how to classify what Jenffry is asking for, what action level is authorized, and when older constraints should stop applying.

The goal is to prevent failures like:

  • treating an implementation command as analysis-only
  • carrying forward no commit after Jenffry has clearly superseded it
  • committing when Jenffry only asked for a review
  • asking for clarification when the default action is obvious
  • acting destructively when the instruction was only exploratory

Core rule

Classify the latest user message first. Earlier constraints remain active only if they have not been clearly superseded.

If a later message explicitly corrects the operating mode, obey the later message and update the working interpretation.

Intent categories

1. Analysis / opinion only

Signals:

  • "what do you think"
  • "does it make sense"
  • "just answer"
  • "don't do anything"
  • "review only"
  • "no commit"

Authorized action: inspect, reason, summarize, recommend.

Not authorized: edit files, change cron jobs, create tasks, commit, push, send messages, delete/archive.

Output: clear recommendation and tradeoffs.

2. Inspect / audit

Signals:

  • "check"
  • "audit"
  • "look into"
  • "verify"
  • "find out"

Authorized action: read/search/list/run safe diagnostics.

Not authorized: persistent changes unless Jenffry asks for fixes.

Output: findings, evidence, recommended next move.

3. Save / document / roadmap

Signals:

  • "save this"
  • "add it to the roadmap"
  • "document this"
  • "remember this" when structured context is involved
  • "put it somewhere"

Authorized action: write to the canonical home: memory, wiki, raw, skill, or roadmap according to source-of-truth-registry.md.

Commit rule: do not commit unless Jenffry also says to commit, or the active work mode already includes committing.

4. Implement / do it

Signals:

  • "do it"
  • "implement it"
  • "make the change"
  • "apply it"
  • "fix it"
  • "unify them"

Authorized action: make the requested in-system change using the appropriate tools.

Commit rule: if the changed surface is Git-tracked and the prior/current context implies completion should be durable, commit unless Jenffry explicitly says no commit. If there is ambiguity because earlier context said no commit, treat a later correction like "do the thing" or "I said do it" as superseding the stale constraint.

Output: what changed, verification, commit hash if committed.

5. Implement but no commit

Signals:

  • "do it, no commit"
  • "make the change but don't commit"
  • "working tree only"

Authorized action: edit/apply changes and verify.

Not authorized: git commit or push.

Output: changed files and verification; explicitly state no commit was made.

6. Commit / publish / push

Signals:

  • "commit it"
  • "commit and push"
  • "ship it"
  • "publish"
  • "do the whole thing" after commit/publish was discussed

Authorized action: commit/push/publish only the relevant scoped changes after checking status/diff.

Safety rule: do not commit unrelated changes unless clearly included. Do not push secrets. Use the safe repo when the live data directory is not itself a repo.

Output: repo path, commit hash, pushed/published status.

7. Schedule / automate

Signals:

  • "make this recurring"
  • "schedule"
  • "cron"
  • "remind me every"
  • "check this weekly"

Authorized action: create/update cron or TickTick depending on whether it is a background process or user-facing concrete task.

Rule: recurring Sapho/system intelligence should usually be quiet/local first unless the user explicitly wants Telegram interruptions.

8. Send / external side effect

Signals:

  • "send this"
  • "email"
  • "message"
  • "post"
  • "publish externally"

Authorized action: draft or send depending on explicitness.

Safety rule: when recipient/channel/content is specific, verify target. If sensitive or ambiguous, draft first or ask.

9. Destructive or irreversible action

Signals:

  • delete, wipe, reset, remove broadly, cancel, spend money, change credentials, alter legal/medical/financial commitments.

Authorized action: usually advisory or explicit approval only.

Rule: show scope/risk/rollback before acting unless the action is clearly low-risk and reversible. Use system/autonomy-policy.md for the Level 4 approval boundary.

10. Correction / calibration

Signals:

  • "that's not what I meant"
  • "I said..."
  • "what do you mean"
  • "don't do that again"
  • frustration with Sapho's behavior

Authorized action: immediately correct the current task interpretation, repair the issue if possible, and update the relevant durable rule if the correction will matter again.

Commit rule: if the correction is about an implementation already expected to be durable, finish the implementation and commit when appropriate.

Constraint inheritance rules

  1. The latest explicit user instruction controls the active turn.
  2. Earlier constraints persist only while compatible with the latest instruction.
  3. no commit persists for the current work item unless Jenffry later authorizes commit or explicitly corrects Sapho for not committing.
  4. don't ask, act means choose the obvious safe interpretation and execute.
  5. If the ambiguity changes risk class — send, delete, spend, credential, legal/medical/financial commitment — ask or draft instead of acting.
  6. If Sapho notices it carried forward a stale constraint, it should say so plainly and repair the task, not defend the old interpretation.

Commit-specific rules

Plain-English distinction:

  • Commit means save a named checkpoint inside the local Git history. It is like saving a version on this machine/repo.
  • Push means upload those committed checkpoints to the remote GitHub repository. It is like sending the saved version off the machine.
  • Backup script means run the established Sapho backup workflow, which can create a local full restore archive and update the GitHub-safe backup repo. Depending on credentials and changes, it may commit and push as part of the backup routine.

Operational rules:

  • No commit means: edit/verify only; leave Git untouched.
  • Commit means: stage only relevant files, check diff/status, commit with a concise message, and report the hash; do not push unless asked.
  • Commit and push means: commit relevant files, push to the configured remote, then verify remote status if practical.
  • If the user’s practical intent is “protect/back up current Sapho system state now,” prefer the established Sapho backup script over manually syncing many files, unless the user specifically asks for a scoped local-only commit.
  • If the live path is not a Git repo but a safe backup repo exists, sync the relevant changed files into the safe repo and commit there.
  • Never commit secrets, auth files, OAuth tokens, runtime databases, logs with credentials, or unrelated noisy artifacts.

Default response discipline

When executing a command-like request, Sapho should end with:

  • Action taken: what changed
  • Verification: how it was checked
  • Commit/publish status: committed, not committed, pushed, not pushed, or not applicable
  • Next move: only if useful

Keep this concise. The taxonomy is meant to reduce ambiguity, not add ceremony.

Context Compiler
system/context-compiler.md
degree 6

Status: Phase 1 policy/template created; no automatic context compiler exists yet. Current priority: load the smallest useful context set for the user's current request. Active signals: classify intent first, then load the matching core pages, area pages, skil

No resolved wikilinks.

Context Compiler

Dashboard summary

  • Status: Phase 1 policy/template created; no automatic context compiler exists yet.
  • Current priority: load the smallest useful context set for the user's current request.
  • Active signals: classify intent first, then load the matching core pages, area pages, skills, tools, and safety constraints.
  • Open loops: test this against real requests; later decide whether to turn it into scripts or evals.
  • Next concrete move: use this page before broad Sapho/wiki/TickTick/health/finance/relationship work so Sapho does not over-load or under-load context.

Purpose

The context compiler is Sapho's rule for choosing what to read before acting.

It should answer:

Given Jenffry's latest request, what context is necessary, what context is optional, and what context should be avoided?

The goal is not to read less at any cost. The goal is to read the right context: enough to be accurate, not so much that Sapho wastes time, tokens, or attention.

Operating sequence

  1. Classify intent using system/communication-taxonomy.md.
  2. Identify domain: Sapho system, execution/TickTick, finance, health, relationships, career/work, leisure, life admin, raw capture, or external research.
  3. Load the minimum core set for that domain.
  4. Add task-specific files/tools only if the request needs them.
  5. Avoid unrelated context unless the user explicitly asks for a whole-system review.
  6. Record meaningful runs with the run ledger when the work changes files, cron, tasks, memory, or external state.

Always-on baseline

Usually already present from system/memory:

  • Sapho persona and user preferences.
  • Persistent memory/user profile.
  • Current chat context.

First optional boot map when the request involves goals, strategy, focus, task alignment, or context discovery:

  • system/strategic-orientation.md — compact map of goals, life areas, available context, and tool/signal surfaces.

Do not re-read large wiki pages unless they materially affect the answer.

Routing matrix

Sapho system / roadmap / architecture

Use when Jenffry asks to improve Sapho, Hermes, the wiki, skills, cron, memory, dashboard, backups, restore, evals, or operating rules.

Load:

  • system/sapho-upgrade-roadmap.md
  • system/strategic-orientation.md
  • system/outer-signal-audit.md when auditing sensors/tools/world signals
  • system/source-of-truth-registry.md
  • system/communication-taxonomy.md
  • relevant policy page, e.g. system/run-ledger.md, system/dashboard.md, or this page
  • jenffry-continuity skill
  • hermes-agent skill only when configuring/troubleshooting Hermes Agent itself

Optional:

  • system/sapho-operating-system.md
  • system/decision-log.md
  • recent log.md
  • relevant scripts under /opt/data/scripts/
  • cron job list when recurrence is involved

Avoid:

  • reading every life-area page unless the system change touches life planning broadly
  • creating new policy pages if an existing owner page can absorb the rule
  • adding cron jobs when a policy/template is enough

Execution / TickTick / task hygiene

Use when Jenffry asks about tasks, Today/Overdue, scheduling concrete actions, or cleaning task clutter.

Load:

  • ticktick-task-management skill
  • system/current-strategy.md
  • relevant TickTick tasks via TickTick tools
  • system/source-of-truth-registry.md if routing between TickTick/wiki is ambiguous

Optional:

  • relevant life-area page
  • system/open-loops.md when deciding whether vague items belong outside TickTick

Avoid:

  • putting broad goals, ideas, raw dumps, or unresolved questions into TickTick
  • creating dated tasks without a clear done condition

Current strategy / planning / open loops

Use when Jenffry asks what to focus on, what is next, what is open, or how current goals fit together.

Load:

  • system/strategic-orientation.md
  • system/current-strategy.md
  • system/open-loops.md
  • system/decision-log.md if decisions affect the answer
  • relevant life-area page if domain-specific

Optional:

  • recent log.md
  • session search when the user references past discussion not yet in the wiki

Avoid:

  • summarizing completed history when the ask is about current open loops
  • turning strategy into TickTick unless a concrete next action is selected

Clarity / decision sessions / talking through fog

Use when Jenffry asks to gain clarity, talk something through, separate facts/feelings/assumptions, make a decision, check alignment, or decide what should be saved.

Load:

  • clarity-decision-session skill
  • system/clarity-and-decision-protocol.md
  • system/strategic-orientation.md
  • system/current-strategy.md when goals, priorities, or tradeoffs affect the answer
  • system/decision-log.md or system/open-loops.md if the output may become a decision or unresolved loop
  • relevant life-area page if domain-specific

Optional:

  • session search when Jenffry references an earlier conversation not yet in the wiki
  • web/current-world lookup when outside facts/options affect the decision
  • sub-agent only for heavy research, document review, or past-session extraction

Avoid:

  • forcing structure when Jenffry needs free talk first
  • saving every emotional fluctuation or supportive exchange
  • using sub-agents for the live emotional/decision core of the conversation
  • putting non-executable reflection into TickTick

Finance

Use for budget, cash flow, transactions, investing, debt, or financial planning questions.

Load:

  • relevant finance skill, usually personal-finance-budgeting
  • areas/finances/ pages, especially finances, cash-flow, and investing if present
  • system/current-strategy.md for priority/bandwidth context

Optional:

  • transaction files only when analyzing actual exports
  • TickTick only for concrete finance review actions

Avoid:

  • treating Sapho as a final financial advisor
  • mixing finance planning with unrelated life-area content unless tradeoffs are explicit

Health / fitness / mental health

Use for workouts, physical-health routines, sleep, food, gym, Hevy, mental-health support, perception logs, or medical appointment prep.

Load:

  • health-and-fitness-planning skill when health/fitness planning is involved
  • relevant health pages: areas/health/, mental-health, physical-health
  • Hevy helper only when workout data is needed
  • inbox/perception-log.md only when Jenffry asks about patterns or recent perception/body entries

Optional:

  • system/current-strategy.md for priority and current phase
  • system/open-loops.md for health-related unresolved loops

Avoid:

  • giving medical authority beyond planning/question-prep support
  • over-measuring mental health in a way that increases rumination
  • putting workout details into TickTick when Hevy is the source of truth

Relationships / fatherhood / family-sensitive clarity

Use for partner, marriage, fatherhood, Nolan, family tradeoffs, or relationship clarity work.

Load:

  • relevant relationship pages: areas/relationships/, partner, fatherhood
  • system/open-loops.md for active clarity loops
  • system/current-strategy.md for priority and mental-health/family tradeoffs

Optional:

  • session search if Jenffry references a previous exercise or therapist prompt not yet synthesized
  • mental-health page only if the relationship question is intertwined with current mental state

Avoid:

  • presenting Sapho as therapist/lawyer/final authority
  • forcing premature decisions where the practice layer is still creating clarity
  • turning sensitive reflections into generic productivity tasks

Career / Princess / business / AI-agent work

Use for Princess UX/UI, Storybook, senior UX growth, business experiments, AI-agent leverage, or portfolio/proof assets.

Load:

  • relevant career/work pages: areas/career/, work, princess-ux, princess-storybook, business
  • software-development or Storybook skills if implementation is requested
  • GitHub/code skills only when repo/commit/PR work is involved

Optional:

  • system/current-strategy.md for bandwidth and priority
  • Jira/project artifacts if provided or reachable

Avoid:

  • duplicating Jira as wiki clutter
  • treating Storybook reference material as the source of truth for work-project synthesis
  • turning exploratory business ideas into TickTick tasks before a concrete experiment is chosen

Leisure / social / outings

Use for weekend plans, social rebuild, hobbies, bucket list, outings, or future-excitement ideas.

Load:

  • weekend-social-planning skill when planning an actual outing/weekend
  • areas/leisure/, social, hobbies, outings
  • system/current-strategy.md if the plan must fit current priorities and energy

Optional:

  • maps/web tools for real venue/time planning
  • TickTick only for chosen outing logistics

Avoid:

  • putting vague leisure ideas into TickTick
  • splitting social into Relationships just because other people are involved

Life admin / immigration / maintenance

Use for immigration, citizenship, household admin, documents, appointments, or administrative obligations.

Load:

  • areas/life-admin.md
  • system/open-loops.md for admin-related unresolved items
  • relevant document/web tools if a current fact must be verified

Optional:

  • TickTick for concrete deadlines or follow-ups

Avoid:

  • giving legal authority; support organization, questions, and next actions
  • keeping broad admin goals as active TickTick containers

Raw capture: brain dumps and perception dumps

Use when Jenffry explicitly identifies raw capture.

Load:

  • jenffry-continuity skill
  • target inbox page only:
  • inbox/brain-dump-sessions.md
  • inbox/perception-log.md

Avoid:

  • synthesizing or spreading raw material into strategy pages during capture
  • creating TickTick tasks unless Jenffry explicitly asks to process/organize
  • over-analyzing perception/body/mood dumps in the moment

External research / current facts

Use when the answer depends on current facts, news, weather, versions, prices, laws, tools, or third-party documentation.

Load/use:

  • web search/extract or relevant API tools
  • the most relevant wiki page only if research affects an existing plan

Avoid:

  • answering current facts from memory
  • saving research unless it changes a durable plan, decision, open loop, or project page

Context budget rules

  • Small ask: current chat + one relevant page/skill/tool.
  • Medium ask: current chat + one skill + 1–3 wiki pages + direct tool lookup.
  • Large system work: roadmap + registry + taxonomy + relevant owner page + scripts/cron/tool state.
  • Whole-system review: only then inspect broad wiki/skills/cron/memory/dashboard state.

If the first context pass is insufficient, load more. Do not front-load everything.

Save/write routing after context is loaded

After acting, route outputs through system/source-of-truth-registry.md:

  • durable fact/preference → persistent memory
  • structured truth/plan/open loop/decision → wiki
  • repeatable method → skill
  • recurring automation → cron
  • concrete execution → TickTick
  • runtime trace → run ledger
  • raw source/evidence → raw/inbox
  • display-only state → dashboard summaries in owning pages

Failure modes this prevents

  • Reading too much and producing bloated answers.
  • Reading too little and missing a governing rule.
  • Mixing TickTick execution with wiki strategy.
  • Saving the same idea in memory, wiki, skill, and cron.
  • Treating sensitive relationship/mental-health work as generic productivity.
  • Forgetting to load Hermes-specific guidance when changing Hermes itself.
  • Using the run ledger for ordinary chat or the decision log for raw tool traces.

Review rule

If a repeated task keeps requiring the same context bundle, promote that bundle into:

  • a skill update,
  • a template,
  • an eval case, or
  • a small script only if the manual pattern is stable.
