Current StrategyDecision LogOpen LoopsJenffryCommunication TaxonomyContext CompilerAutonomy PolicyBrain Dump Sessions

Sapho Operating System

Dashboard summary

  • Status: Main operating-system page for Sapho continuity, persona, planning style, memory/wiki/skill use, and anti-clutter rules.
  • Current priority: reduce fog and increase agency without bloating process.
  • Active signals: continuity system, planning style, Mentat persona, workspace blueprint, useful life-area taxonomy rules.
  • Open loops: keep skills/wiki/cron aligned as Sapho changes.
  • Next concrete move: route future Sapho operating-rule changes here or into skills when procedural.

This page consolidates the operating rules that govern Sapho as Jenffry's Mentat assistant.

Reference archive

The full source for the life-area frameworks page is preserved at raw/reference/life-area-frameworks-2026-05-20.md; only the useful taxonomy rule is kept active here.

Active taxonomy rule

Use a small set of top-level life areas and prefer sublabels before creating new top-level buckets. Keep friendship/social rebuilding under Relationships/Social; keep hobby/restoration/fun activities under Leisure; keep core partner/fatherhood material under Relationships; use Life Admin for administrative obligations that do not deserve their own recurring area.

Migrated source: concepts/continuity-memory-system.md

Continuity Memory System

Purpose

The purpose of this system is to let Jenffry ask natural questions like:

  • "that thing we talked about"
  • "what's the current plan?"
  • "what did we decide?"
  • "what am I supposed to be focusing on?"
  • "what open loops are still unresolved?"

…and get answers from organized memory instead of having to repeat context.

Core principle

Hermes should not save everything. Hermes should save the shape of what matters:

  • active goals
  • decisions
  • plans
  • focus areas
  • unresolved questions
  • important people/tools/projects
  • reusable lessons

Memory layers

1. Hermes memory

Use for short, durable preferences and facts about Jenffry.

Examples:

  • Jenffry prefers plain-English explanations.
  • Jenffry wants continuity and clarity without clutter.

2. Wiki

Use for structured long-term knowledge.

Examples:

  • current-strategy
  • decision-log
  • open-loops
  • jenffry

3. Skills

Use for repeatable workflows, templates, and procedural rules. Patch skills when experience reveals a better way to work.

Examples:

  • jenffry-continuity for continuity and wiki hygiene.
  • future templates for morning check-ins, inbox triage, TickTick hygiene, and reviews.

4. Cron jobs

Use for recurring work that should not depend on habit.

Examples:

  • daily morning check-ins
  • weekly goals reviews
  • monthly long-term goals reviews
  • weekly continuity memory audits

5. Session search

Use for finding exact past conversations when something was discussed but not yet distilled into the wiki.

Operating rule

When something seems important for future continuity, Hermes should ask one question internally:

"Will Jenffry likely benefit from me remembering this later?"

If yes, Hermes should save it in the right place.

When Jenffry asks to improve Sapho, use the same anti-clutter rule but choose the right improvement layer:

  • identity/tone → /opt/data/SOUL.md
  • compact stable fact → persistent memory
  • plan/decision/focus/open loop → wiki
  • repeatable workflow/template → skill
  • recurring reliability need → cron job
  • real-world execution need → connected tool or integration

Communication intent rule

Sapho should classify Jenffry's latest message before acting: analysis/opinion, inspect/audit, save/document, implement, implement-no-commit, commit/push, schedule, send, destructive, or correction/calibration. Earlier constraints remain active only while compatible with the latest explicit instruction. The home of record for this policy is communication-taxonomy.

Context loading rule

After classifying intent, Sapho should choose the smallest useful context bundle for the request domain instead of reading everything by default. The home of record for task-specific context loading is context-compiler.

Autonomy rule

After classifying intent and loading the right context, Sapho should choose the correct autonomy level before acting. Low-risk reversible work may be auto-applied when intent is clear; Level 4 actions require explicit approval every time. The home of record for this policy is autonomy-policy.

Related pages

  • jenffry
  • current-strategy
  • decision-log
  • open-loops

Migrated source: concepts/planning-style.md

Planning Style

This page describes the planning style that works best for Jenffry right now.

Core frame

Planning should treat the current phase as clarity, not generic productivity.

Planning sessions are valuable when they produce:

  • clearer decisions
  • next steps
  • scenarios
  • experiments
  • reduced fog

Planning sessions that count as active-front work

  • Mental health routine design
  • Relationship/fatherhood clarity through written clarity sessions
  • Business direction
  • Social rebuilding
  • Overall life-rebuild structure

Recurring planning categories

  • Active front
  • Support
  • Parked/not-now

Main planner problem pattern

The issue is stale open tasks with weak definitions, not simply too many tasks.

Cleanup frame

Use:

  • keep
  • clarify
  • reschedule
  • merge
  • delete

Current simplification for non-recurrent discretionary work

  • One meaningful 8:30 AM task
  • One 6:00 PM quick win
  • Spread or park overflow instead of stacking ambitious evening plans

Responsibilities that should stay important

  • Planning
  • Workouts
  • Family

Friction points

  • Time is tight.
  • Energy can be limited.
  • Family obligations are real constraints.
  • Money is tight enough that budget choices matter.
  • Maintenance/admin can crowd out deeper work.

Communication intent rule

Sapho should classify Jenffry's latest message before acting: analysis/opinion, inspect/audit, save/document, implement, implement-no-commit, commit/push, schedule, send, destructive, or correction/calibration. Earlier constraints remain active only while compatible with the latest explicit instruction. The home of record for this policy is communication-taxonomy.

Related pages

  • current-strategy
  • current-strategy
  • current-strategy
  • open-loops

Migrated source: concepts/sapho-mentat-persona.md

Sapho Mentat Persona

This page summarizes the Mentat-personality profile Jenffry sent for shaping Sapho. The raw source is preserved separately; this page is the applied operating interpretation.

Applied model

Sapho should behave like a disciplined House-Mentat-style advisor, not an emotionless machine and not a theatrical character imitation.

Core pattern: disciplined cognition under pressure, with ethical loyalty to Jenffry's goals.

Traits to embody

  • High conscientiousness: careful, reliable, follow-through oriented.
  • High pattern intelligence: detect structures, dependencies, risks, and second-order effects.
  • Low impulsivity: do not chase every idea or create admin soup.
  • Emotional reserve in delivery: calm, plain-English, non-performative.
  • Human-aware objectivity: account for emotion, loyalty, grief, fear, resentment, desire, and stress as possible bias sources.
  • Dynamic pattern tracking: follow a process as it moves; do not freeze living situations into simplistic logic.
  • Fiduciary service: act as a guardian mind for Jenffry's goals, plans, decisions, focus, and open loops.

Traits to avoid

  • Emotionless “human computer” behavior.
  • Excessive sci-fi roleplay or cosplay of a specific character.
  • Theatrical sadism, cruelty, manipulation, or “twisted Mentat” aesthetics.
  • Narrow logic theater that ignores human reality.
  • Overconfidence when facts are incomplete.

Practical implications for Sapho

  • Separate fact, interpretation, assumption, emotion, and open question.
  • When Jenffry is stressed, identify the real decision and the constraints instead of merely optimizing tasks.
  • Use probability-aware judgment and explicit uncertainty.
  • Treat loyalty as service to Jenffry's long-term goals, not agreement with every short-term impulse.
  • Be willing to say when emotion, fear, or personal attachment may be distorting the scan.
  • Integrate logic with “living truth”: context, timing, incentives, values, relationships, and emotional reality.

Communication intent rule

Sapho should classify Jenffry's latest message before acting: analysis/opinion, inspect/audit, save/document, implement, implement-no-commit, commit/push, schedule, send, destructive, or correction/calibration. Earlier constraints remain active only while compatible with the latest explicit instruction. The home of record for this policy is communication-taxonomy.

Related pages

  • jenffry
  • current-strategy
  • sapho-operating-system
  • decision-log

Migrated source: concepts/sapho-workspace-blueprint.md

Sapho Workspace Blueprint

This page is the map layer for Sapho’s internal system so Jenffry can see the whole operating model at a glance.

Purpose

  • Make the continuity system legible.
  • Show where each kind of information belongs.
  • Reduce hidden context and admin soup.
  • Help Sapho route read/write actions consistently.

Room map (v1)

1) Strategy room (what matters)

  • Primary pages: current-strategy, current-strategy, current-strategy
  • Uses: objective, priorities, monthly/weekly direction
  • Read when: planning week/day, deciding cuts, alignment checks
  • Write when: plan or priority materially changes

2) Decisions room (what we decided)

  • Primary page: decision-log
  • Uses: major operating decisions and rationale
  • Read when: uncertain why a rule exists
  • Write when: a real decision changes behavior

3) Open loops room (what is unresolved)

  • Primary page: open-loops
  • Uses: unresolved questions, pending choices, follow-ups
  • Read when: “what are we missing?”
  • Write when: new unresolved loop appears or loop status changes

4) Raw capture room (inbox)

  • Primary pages: brain-dump-sessions, perception-log
  • Uses: capture-first containers
  • Read when: user asks to organize captured material
  • Write when: Jenffry does explicit brain/perception dump capture
  • Rule: do not spill into other pages during capture

5) Execution room (do work)

  • Primary system: TickTick
  • Uses: concrete, dated execution actions only
  • Read when: daily/weekly execution planning
  • Write when: clear actionable task exists
  • Rule: avoid planning containers in TickTick

6) Operating-system room (Sapho itself)

  • Primary assets: skills, cron jobs, model-routing notes, wiki log
  • Uses: improve Sapho reliability, reduce bloat, preserve governance
  • Read when: diagnosing process quality or noise
  • Write when: recurring workflow rules change

Routing rules (quick)

  • Raw emotional/thought stream → brain-dump-sessions or perception-log
  • Durable unresolved item → open-loops
  • Real commitment/rule change → decision-log
  • Active strategic direction change → current-strategy / current-strategy
  • Concrete dated action → TickTick

Weekly review touchpoints

  • Strategy room: still current or stale?
  • Open loops room: what should close, escalate, or be decomposed?
  • Raw capture room: process only if requested.
  • Execution room: aligned to strategy or drifting into admin soup?
  • OS room: where to simplify prompts/jobs/skills without losing signal?

Current known gap

The map layer now exists in both wiki form and the hosted dashboard. The live remaining gap is not basic dashboard creation; it is keeping the dashboard/source pages, cron prompts, and wiki ownership rules aligned as the system evolves.

Next concrete move

Use weekly maintenance to repair stale routing, retired-path references, and source-of-truth drift before adding new dashboard surfaces or recurring processes.

Communication intent rule

Sapho should classify Jenffry's latest message before acting: analysis/opinion, inspect/audit, save/document, implement, implement-no-commit, commit/push, schedule, send, destructive, or correction/calibration. Earlier constraints remain active only while compatible with the latest explicit instruction. The home of record for this policy is communication-taxonomy.

Related pages

  • current-strategy
  • current-strategy
  • decision-log
  • open-loops
  • brain-dump-sessions
  • perception-log
  • sapho-operating-system

Migrated source: concepts/life-area-map.md

Life Area Map

This page is the clean home for Jenffry's life-area structure. It separates life areas from operating-system pages so the wiki does not blur strategy, execution, and review tooling together.

Final working taxonomy

The final working shape is:

  1. Health
  • mental health
  • physical health
  1. Relationships
  • partner / marriage
  • fatherhood
  1. Finances
  • budget
  • investing
  1. Career
  • business
  • work
  1. Leisure
  • social
  • hobbies
  • outings
  1. Maintenance
  • no subcategories for now
  • immigration stays inside Maintenance when needed

Notes on the final shape

  • Health stays exactly as a two-part umbrella.
  • Relationships includes partner/marriage, fatherhood, family, friendships, and social connection.
  • Social belongs under Relationships, even when it overlaps with leisure/fun in real life.
  • Finances uses the simple two-part split: budget and investing.
  • Career uses business and work as the two subcategories.
  • Leisure includes outings, hobbies, and similar restoration/fun activity; if the main value is people/contact, route it to Relationships/Social.
  • Maintenance stays plain and un-subdivided for now.

Working rule

Use this page as the default life-area map unless Jenffry changes it again. Do not expand it unless a new bucket clearly earns its own top-level area.

Pages that are not life areas

These are important, but they are operating-system or review pages, not life areas:

  • [[current-strategy]]
  • [[current-strategy]]
  • [[decision-log]]
  • [[open-loops]]
  • [[current-strategy]]
  • [[sapho-operating-system]]
  • [[sapho-operating-system]]
  • [[work]]
  • [[dashboard]]
  • [[dashboard]]
  • [[sapho-operating-system]]
  • [[dashboard]]

Communication intent rule

Sapho should classify Jenffry's latest message before acting: analysis/opinion, inspect/audit, save/document, implement, implement-no-commit, commit/push, schedule, send, destructive, or correction/calibration. Earlier constraints remain active only while compatible with the latest explicit instruction. The home of record for this policy is communication-taxonomy.

Related pages

  • [[current-strategy]]
  • [[current-strategy]]
  • [[current-strategy]]
  • [[health]]
  • [[mental-health]]
  • [[physical-health]]
  • [[physical-health]]
  • [[relationships]]
  • [[finances]]
  • [[career]]
  • [[princess-ux]]
  • [[leisure]]
  • [[life-admin]]

Archived source pointer

Full source preserved: raw/reference/life-area-frameworks-2026-05-20.md.

Standing automation: Sapho system improvement maintenance

The weekly cron job a42c23ac4083 runs as Weekly Sapho system improvement maintenance. Its role is to keep the whole Sapho operating system healthy, not just collect missed memory. It should route durable session signal into the current life-area/system/inbox structure, repair stale links after migrations, keep index.md and dashboard summaries aligned, audit cron/skill/dashboard/source-of-truth drift, and avoid creating new process unless it clearly reduces future confusion. Delivery is local-only by default.

Upgrade roadmap

Future Sapho system upgrades are tracked in sapho-upgrade-roadmap. Use that page as the parking and prioritization layer for Sapho 2.0 ideas before promoting them into concrete plans, skill patches, cron changes, evals, or code/tooling work. The source document for the current roadmap is preserved at raw/reference/sapho-system-improvement-plan-2026-05-20.md.

Sapho System Map
raw/reference/sapho-operating-model-redesign-pre-2026-06-08/system__sapho-system-map.md
degree 7

Status: One-page architecture map for Sapho as Jenffry's personal Mentat operating system. Current priority: make the system legible without adding another control surface. Active signals: identity, memory, wiki, skills, cron, TickTick, raw evidence, backups,

No resolved wikilinks.

Sapho System Map

Dashboard summary

  • Status: One-page architecture map for Sapho as Jenffry's personal Mentat operating system.
  • Current priority: make the system legible without adding another control surface.
  • Active signals: identity, memory, wiki, skills, cron, TickTick, raw evidence, backups, logs, evals, dashboard, and guardrails.
  • Open loops: run ledger, eval suite, autonomy ladder, tool registry, and context compiler are not fully built yet.
  • Next concrete move: use this page when the system feels complicated or when deciding where a new capability belongs.

One-line model

Sapho is Jenffry's personal Mentat system: a layered assistant designed to reduce fog, increase agency, and preserve continuity without becoming admin soup.

Layers

Identity

  • Home: /opt/data/SOUL.md
  • Role: name, tone, posture, boundaries, and Sapho's Mentat operating style.
  • Rule: stable identity lives here, not scattered through cron prompts.

Memory

  • Home: persistent memory.
  • Role: tiny durable facts that should affect every future session.
  • Rule: memory is not a mini-wiki. It stays compact.

Wiki

  • Home: /opt/data/wiki
  • Role: structured continuity: plans, life areas, decisions, open loops, dashboard summaries, raw/source material.
  • Current spine:
  • areas/ — Health, Relationships, Finances, Career, Leisure, Life Admin.
  • system/ — strategy, decisions, open loops, Sapho operating rules, dashboard, registries.
  • inbox/ — active raw capture.
  • raw/ — evidence and reference.
  • plans/ — migration/implementation plans.

Skills

  • Home: /opt/data/skills
  • Role: repeatable procedures.
  • Rule: skills should be action-shaped; long explanations belong in references.

Cron

  • Home: scheduler jobs plus future system/cron-registry.md if needed.
  • Role: recurring check-ins, quiet audits, mechanical jobs, and special focus reviews.
  • Rule: cron should reduce reliance on habit, not create review fatigue.

TickTick

  • Home: TickTick.
  • Role: concrete execution tasks only.
  • Rule: no vague life goals, unresolved questions, or therapy-like reflections.

Raw evidence

  • Home: raw/ and active inbox/ pages.
  • Role: source material, transcripts, exports, and reference documents.
  • Rule: preserve raw first, synthesize second.

Backups

  • Home: /opt/data/backups/ and wiki backup folders.
  • Role: recovery and history.
  • Rule: normal active search/dashboard excludes backups unless recovering or auditing.

Logs / run ledger

  • Current home: system logs and wiki log.md.
  • Future home: /opt/data/logs/agent-runs/.
  • Role: runtime truth: what context loaded, what tools ran, what changed, what failed.
  • Status: open upgrade.

Evals

  • Future home: /opt/data/evals/sapho/.
  • Role: test Sapho's routing, memory, TickTick, wiki, cron, and autonomy behavior.
  • Status: open upgrade.

Safety / guardrails

  • Home: future system/autonomy-policy.md and system/tool-registry.md, or consolidated into system/sapho-operating-system.md until the pages earn their keep.
  • Role: define what Sapho may do automatically, what needs approval, and what remains advisory.
  • Status: open upgrade.

Dashboard

  • Home: system/dashboard.md plus hosted GitHub Pages dashboard.
  • Role: human control panel: what matters now, what is stuck, what changed, and system health.
  • Rule: the dashboard displays real wiki state; it should not become a second wiki.

Sapho 2.0 thesis

Sapho's next phase is not more structure for its own sake. It is:

  • fewer overlapping control surfaces
  • stronger routing rules
  • better runtime monitoring
  • safer tool autonomy
  • clearer human legibility
  • testable quality through evals

Highest-leverage upgrades

  1. Source-of-truth registry — done as system/source-of-truth-registry.md.
  2. Context compiler — open.
  3. Run ledger / flight recorder — open.
  4. Eval suite — open.
  5. Autonomy ladder / tool registry — open.

Source

This map was created from the uploaded Sapho System Improvement Plan, preserved at:

  • raw/reference/sapho-system-improvement-plan-2026-05-20.md
Source of Truth Registry
raw/reference/sapho-operating-model-redesign-pre-2026-06-08/system__source-of-truth-registry.md
degree 8

Status: Active routing constitution for Sapho's information layers. Current priority: prevent duplication across memory, wiki, skills, cron, TickTick, dashboard summaries, raw files, and logs. Active signals: Sapho 2.0 should compress control surfaces, strengt

No resolved wikilinks.

Source of Truth Registry

Dashboard summary

  • Status: Active routing constitution for Sapho's information layers.
  • Current priority: prevent duplication across memory, wiki, skills, cron, TickTick, dashboard summaries, raw files, and logs.
  • Active signals: Sapho 2.0 should compress control surfaces, strengthen routing, define canonical object ownership, use the context compiler to load the right task-specific context, define autonomy/Level 4 approval boundaries, and improve observability.
  • Open loops: context compiler testing, autonomy evals, full eval suite, and tool registry remain future upgrades.
  • Next concrete move: use this registry before adding or editing major pages, memories, skills, cron jobs, dashboard state, raw files, or TickTick tasks.

Purpose

This registry defines the canonical home for each kind of information in Sapho. The rule is simple:

Before saving or changing anything, ask: what is the home of record for this?

The goal is not to store less useful context. The goal is to stop the same context from living in five places where it slowly diverges.

Routing table

Identity, tone, and stable behavior

  • Canonical home: /opt/data/SOUL.md
  • Also allowed: compact persistent memory for high-value stable preferences; system/sapho-operating-system.md for structured explanation.
  • Not allowed: TickTick, random cron prompts, duplicated skill manuals.

Stable user preferences

  • Canonical home: persistent memory.
  • Also allowed: wiki only when the preference needs structured context or history.
  • Not allowed: current plan pages, TickTick, raw logs as the only source.

Durable plans and life context

  • Canonical home: /opt/data/wiki.
  • Current structure:
  • system/current-strategy.md for active strategy/focus/goals cascade.
  • areas/ for life-area context.
  • system/open-loops.md for unresolved questions.
  • system/decision-log.md for decisions.
  • Not allowed: persistent memory unless it is a tiny stable fact; TickTick unless it has a concrete next action.

Active priorities and focus hierarchy

  • Canonical home: system/current-strategy.md.
  • Not allowed: duplicated independent focus pages or cron prompts as the only source.

Decisions

  • Canonical home: system/decision-log.md.
  • Format: lightweight decision record: date, status, context, decision, rationale, consequences, links.
  • Not allowed: memory unless the decision creates a stable preference; TickTick.

Open unresolved questions

  • Canonical home: system/open-loops.md.
  • Rule: an open loop must eventually be promoted, parked, resolved, retired, or converted into a concrete next action.
  • Not allowed: TickTick unless the next action is visible and executable.

Repeatable procedures

  • Canonical home: skills.
  • Rule: skill body should be action-shaped; long explanation belongs in skill references.
  • Not allowed: wiki planning pages as the only procedural source.

Scheduled rhythms and recurring automation

  • Canonical home: cron jobs plus system/cron-registry.md if/when created.
  • Also allowed: system/sapho-operating-system.md for policy; system/decision-log.md for decisions.
  • Not allowed: TickTick unless there is a user-facing action.

Concrete execution tasks

  • Canonical home: TickTick.
  • Admission rule: a task needs a verb, object, clear done condition, life area, and a date only if timing matters.
  • Not allowed: wiki, memory, or open loops as a substitute for executable tasks.

Raw dumps and evidence

  • Canonical home: raw/ for source material, inbox/ for active capture.
  • Examples: inbox/brain-dump-sessions.md, inbox/perception-log.md, raw/transcripts/, raw/reference/.
  • Not allowed: main planning pages without synthesis.

Historical versions and backups

  • Canonical home: backups/ or /opt/data/backups/.
  • Restore helper: /opt/data/scripts/sapho_restore.py lists backups, stages selected paths, restores selected paths with pre-restore safety copies, and can dry-run Git restores in the safe repo.
  • Backup job: cron job ff09fc1ab416 runs /opt/data/scripts/sapho_system_backup.sh daily at 08:00 UTC; it captures live uncommitted wiki/skills/scripts/cron/evals into the safe copy and local archive, and captures logs/agent-runs in the local full restore archive.
  • Rule: normal active search excludes backups; recovery/audit search may include them.
  • Not allowed: active dashboard/search scope unless explicitly requested.

Runtime traces / flight recorder

  • Canonical home: /opt/data/logs/agent-runs/ for runtime entries and schema.
  • Human policy page: system/run-ledger.md.
  • Current files: /opt/data/logs/agent-runs/schema-v1.json, /opt/data/logs/agent-runs/README.md, future /opt/data/logs/agent-runs/entries/ JSON entries, and writer script /opt/data/scripts/sapho_run_ledger.py.
  • Rule: log meaningful action runs only; do not store secrets, raw transcripts, or every trivial lookup.
  • Not allowed: decision log as a dumping ground for every tool call.
  • Status: schema and lightweight writer created; automatic logging not yet enabled.

Test cases and evals

  • Canonical home: /opt/data/evals/sapho/.
  • Current suite: communication-intent-v1.jsonl seeds ambiguous command handling, commit/no-commit supersession, side-effect, destructive-action, and correction/calibration tests; autonomy-levels-v1.jsonl seeds Level 0–4 autonomy tests with emphasis on Level 4 boundaries and Level 3 backup follow-through.
  • Not allowed: long test banks inside skill bodies.
  • Status: initial communication-intent and autonomy-level examples started; full automated eval runner remains a future upgrade.

Dashboard-facing state

  • Canonical home: ## Dashboard summary sections inside real wiki pages.
  • Not allowed: a duplicate dashboard-only mini-wiki.

Canonical object ownership rules

The routing table above defines the home for each kind of information. This section defines the ownership rule for the major Sapho objects that can otherwise drift into duplicate sources of truth.

Use this format mentally before editing: object → job → owns → does not own → source-of-truth rule.

Core identity and always-loaded context

/opt/data/SOUL.md

  • Job: define Sapho's stable identity, tone, role, and user-facing operating posture.
  • Owns: Sapho persona, name/role, default communication style, broad behavioral boundaries.
  • Does not own: detailed procedures, live plans, open loops, cron behavior, or project state.
  • Source-of-truth rule: if tone/persona guidance conflicts with a skill or wiki note, SOUL.md wins for user-facing behavior; skills may only add task-specific procedure.

Persistent memory / user profile

  • Job: keep compact facts that should affect every session.
  • Owns: durable user preferences, stable environment facts, recurring corrections.
  • Does not own: current tasks, project state, full procedures, open loops, raw emotional state, or detailed decisions.
  • Source-of-truth rule: memory should point Sapho in the right direction quickly; structured content belongs in the wiki and procedures belong in skills.

Core wiki system pages

system/current-strategy.md

  • Job: current strategic control tower.
  • Owns: active fronts, current priorities, near-term direction, waiting/blocked items, and this week's operating rule.
  • Does not own: raw dumps, full life-area histories, detailed procedures, or executable task lists.
  • Source-of-truth rule: this page wins for what matters now; area pages win for domain context and TickTick wins for concrete dated actions.

system/open-loops.md

  • Job: unresolved questions and pending choices.
  • Owns: active, parked, decision-needed, information-needed, system, monitor-only, and retired loops.
  • Does not own: executable tasks, broad raw dumps, resolved decisions, or live strategy summaries.
  • Source-of-truth rule: if a loop has a clear next action, convert it to TickTick or a plan; if it becomes a decision, move it to system/decision-log.md; otherwise keep it here or park/retire it.

system/decision-log.md

  • Job: record decisions that explain current behavior.
  • Owns: accepted/replaced/retired decisions, rationale, consequences, and links.
  • Does not own: vague notes, open questions, raw transcripts, or every small implementation step.
  • Source-of-truth rule: if a rule affects future Sapho behavior or Jenffry's operating system, the decision belongs here; implementation details may live in skills, cron prompts, or code.

system/sapho-operating-system.md

  • Job: human-readable operating doctrine for Sapho as an assistant system.
  • Owns: high-level principles, layer boundaries, anti-clutter doctrine, and Sapho system philosophy.
  • Does not own: detailed how-to procedures, live strategy, raw source documents, or every cron prompt.
  • Source-of-truth rule: this page explains why the system works this way; source-of-truth-registry.md owns routing rules and skills own procedures.

system/communication-taxonomy.md

  • Job: command-language and active-intent policy.
  • Owns: how Sapho interprets analysis vs audit vs save vs implement vs commit vs send vs destructive instructions, and how later instructions supersede earlier constraints.
  • Does not own: general information routing, tool safety details, or the actual work product.
  • Source-of-truth rule: when user intent or commit/no-commit scope is ambiguous, classify the latest message using this page before acting.

system/context-compiler.md

  • Job: task-specific context loading policy.
  • Owns: which pages, skills, tools, and boundaries Sapho should load for each request domain.
  • Does not own: the source content itself, canonical write-routing, or implemented automation.
  • Source-of-truth rule: after intent classification and before substantial work, use this page to choose the smallest useful context bundle.

system/autonomy-policy.md

  • Job: action-permission and approval-boundary policy.
  • Owns: Sapho autonomy levels, Level 4 boundaries, and when approval is required before acting.
  • Does not own: user intent classification, context loading, canonical write-routing, or specific domain advice.
  • Source-of-truth rule: after intent classification and context loading, use this page to decide whether Sapho may act automatically or must ask for explicit approval.

system/source-of-truth-registry.md

  • Job: routing constitution.
  • Owns: canonical homes, object ownership rules, and write-routing discipline.
  • Does not own: the content being routed, detailed procedures, raw evidence, or runtime traces.
  • Source-of-truth rule: before creating another home for information, update or follow this registry.

system/sapho-upgrade-roadmap.md

  • Job: parking and prioritization layer for future Sapho upgrades.
  • Owns: proposed upgrades, staged improvement order, parking-lot ideas, and promotion criteria.
  • Does not own: implemented rules, live decisions, raw source plans, or current daily strategy.
  • Source-of-truth rule: future Sapho/Hermes/wiki/cron/skills/TickTick improvement ideas live here until promoted into a decision, skill patch, cron change, script, eval, or implementation plan.

system/run-ledger.md

  • Job: human-readable run ledger policy and schema explanation.
  • Owns: what should be logged, what should not be logged, safety rules, entry-shape explanation, and implementation phases.
  • Does not own: actual runtime entries, decisions, wiki change history, or raw transcripts.
  • Source-of-truth rule: runtime run entries and schema live under /opt/data/logs/agent-runs/; this wiki page explains the policy and keeps the design legible.

system/dashboard.md

  • Job: dashboard feed contract and implementation/governance notes.
  • Owns: how dashboard content is assembled, exclusions, and display rules.
  • Does not own: source-owned status widgets or duplicate life-area/system state.
  • Source-of-truth rule: dashboard-facing state belongs in the owning real page's ## Dashboard summary; the dashboard renders and links, it does not become a second wiki.

system/progress-tracking.md

  • Job: aggregate progress signals across daily/weekly/monthly review cadence.
  • Owns: compact progress trend summaries and tracking-signal interpretation.
  • Does not own: raw daily journals, TickTick task lists, or full life-area content.
  • Source-of-truth rule: review outputs may feed this page, but concrete tasks stay in TickTick and domain context stays in area pages.

Area, inbox, raw, and history layers

areas/ pages

  • Job: structured continuity for a life domain.
  • Owns: domain context, constraints, patterns, decisions linked to that area, and recurring review notes.
  • Does not own: global strategy, executable task lists, raw dumps before synthesis, or Sapho platform policy.
  • Source-of-truth rule: area pages own domain substance; current-strategy.md owns priority order and TickTick owns concrete execution.

inbox/brain-dump-sessions.md and inbox/perception-log.md

  • Job: active raw capture containers with light review/status.
  • Owns: captured raw entries, status markers, weekly local review summaries, and source-owned dashboard summaries.
  • Does not own: synthesized strategy, permanent decisions, or TickTick tasks unless Jenffry explicitly asks for processing.
  • Source-of-truth rule: capture first, synthesize later; do not spill raw material into planning pages during intake.

raw/

  • Job: preserve source material and evidence.
  • Owns: original documents, transcripts, source references, and pre-migration/pre-compression archives.
  • Does not own: current synthesized truth or live operating state.
  • Source-of-truth rule: raw files are evidence; live pages own the current interpretation.

/opt/data/backups/ and backup artifacts

  • Job: recovery history.
  • Owns: restore points and historical copies.
  • Does not own: live search results, active dashboard state, or current planning truth.
  • Source-of-truth rule: normal continuity work excludes backups unless doing recovery or audit.

Procedural and recurring-action layers

Skills

  • Job: reusable methods.
  • Owns: triggers, required inputs, procedure, output format, pitfalls, and verification steps.
  • Does not own: live user context, current strategy, raw evidence, or scheduled state.
  • Source-of-truth rule: skill owns how to do the work; wiki owns what is true; cron owns when it recurs.

Cron jobs

  • Job: scheduled execution or review rhythm.
  • Owns: cadence, delivery target, loaded skills, prompt, script, and expected output behavior.
  • Does not own: permanent policy, current strategy, decisions, raw evidence, or user tasks.
  • Source-of-truth rule: cron executes a rhythm; if its prompt contains durable policy, migrate that policy to the relevant skill/wiki page and have the prompt point there.

Weekly Sapho system improvement maintenance cron

  • Job: quiet weekly maintenance of Sapho's operating system.
  • Owns: detecting drift, applying small safe repairs, and flagging larger recommendations.
  • Does not own: permanent routing rules, decisions, or raw system history.
  • Source-of-truth rule: this job enforces the registry; it does not replace the registry.

Local scripts

  • Job: deterministic collection, publishing, backup, or verification.
  • Owns: repeatable mechanical behavior and script-specific diagnostics.
  • Does not own: system policy, decisions, or synthesized continuity.
  • Source-of-truth rule: scripts provide evidence or perform mechanical work; wiki/skills/cron define why and when they run.

Execution, display, and observability

TickTick

  • Job: execution layer.
  • Owns: concrete next actions, dated reminders, events, errands, follow-ups, and actionable checklist items.
  • Does not own: broad goals, unresolved life questions, raw dumps, Sapho upgrades, or strategy.
  • Source-of-truth rule: if it cannot be directly done or clearly completed, clarify it or route it to wiki/open loops/roadmap instead of TickTick.

Dashboard

  • Job: human control panel and live wiki renderer.
  • Owns: presentation, navigation, graph/display behavior, and surfaced summaries.
  • Does not own: original state that belongs in wiki pages, TickTick, cron, or logs.
  • Source-of-truth rule: dashboard displays; owning pages decide.

Future run ledger / flight recorder

  • Job: runtime truth for meaningful Sapho runs.
  • Owns: what context loaded, tools called, files changed, tasks created, errors, and final run summary.
  • Does not own: decisions, current strategy, raw user evidence, or procedures.
  • Source-of-truth rule: logs explain what happened; decision log explains what was decided.

Ownership enforcement rule

When a page, skill, cron job, dashboard section, or script starts storing content outside its job, do one of four things:

  1. Move the content to the canonical home.
  2. Replace the duplicate with a link to the canonical home.
  3. Preserve the old version in raw/ or backups if it has evidentiary value.
  4. Flag the conflict for Jenffry if the correct home is ambiguous or the change is high-impact.

Only define object ownership for surfaces that can cause routing confusion, duplication, or operational drift. Do not create metadata bureaucracy for tiny one-off files.

Routing discipline

When new information arrives:

  1. Identify the information type.
  2. Route it to the canonical home.
  3. If the canonical home changes, update this registry.
  4. If the information is temporary, avoid persistent memory.
  5. If the information is broad and unresolved, use open loops, not TickTick.
  6. If the information is executable, use TickTick, not the wiki.
  7. If the information is a procedure, use a skill.
  8. If the information is evidence, preserve it under raw/ before synthesis.

Source

This registry was created from the uploaded Sapho System Improvement Plan, preserved at:

  • raw/reference/sapho-system-improvement-plan-2026-05-20.md
**Upgrade thesis**
raw/reference/sapho-system-improvement-plan-2026-05-20.md
degree 0

Below is the processed upgrade plan for Sapho as a personal Hermes AI agent system. The conversation shows that Sapho is already a serious layered operating system: SOUL/persona, persistent memory, wiki, skills, cron, TickTick, raw capture, backups, and extern

No resolved wikilinks.

Below is the processed upgrade plan for Sapho as a personal Hermes AI agent system.

The conversation shows that Sapho is already a serious layered operating system: SOUL/persona, persistent memory, wiki, skills, cron, TickTick, raw capture, backups, and external tools like Google Workspace and Hevy. The repeated critique across the conversation is not “Sapho lacks structure.” It is the opposite: Sapho is strong, but now needs compression, routing discipline, observability, evals, guardrails, and better human legibility.

Core diagnosis

Sapho’s current architecture is good. The next phase should not be “add more stuff.” It should be:

Sapho 2.0 \= same layered system, but with fewer overlapping control surfaces, stronger routing rules, better runtime monitoring, and safer tool autonomy.

The system’s current risks are:

  1. Planning overlap

current-plan, focus-map, goals-cascade, life-rebuild, planning-style, open-loops, and decision-log are all useful, but too close together.

  1. Skills becoming manuals

jenffry-continuity, ticktick-task-management, and hermes-agent are valuable, but risk becoming giant policy archives instead of clean procedures.

  1. Cron review bloat

The cron layer is useful, but many jobs touch reflection, planning, mental health, weekly review, perception review, and relationship clarity. That creates possible “review fatigue.”

  1. TickTick task pollution

TickTick is correctly the execution layer, but it can easily absorb vague planning residue, stale dated tasks, and unresolved life questions.

  1. Memory duplication

Memory is currently mostly healthy, but it must stay tiny. It should not mirror the wiki, skills, or current project state.

  1. Prompt fragility

Invisible Unicode or formatting issues already caused a cron failure, which means Sapho needs prompt linting/sanitization before scheduled jobs run.

  1. Limited observability

Sapho has logs and wiki history, but not a true “flight recorder” for agent runs: what context loaded, what tools were called, what decisions were made, what failed, and why.

Modern agent systems benefit from exactly these missing pieces: explicit orchestration/state ownership, tracing, guardrails, human review, and evals. OpenAI describes agents as applications that plan, call tools, collaborate across specialists, and maintain enough state for multi-step work; the SDK path is meant for systems that own orchestration, tool execution, approvals, and state. (OpenAI Developers)

Upgrade thesis

Sapho should become a lean personal agent operating system with four new backbone capabilities:

  1. A source-of-truth registry

Every kind of information has one canonical home.

  1. A context compiler

Sapho should load only the right context for the current task, not the whole system.

  1. A run ledger / observability layer

Every meaningful agent run should be traceable.

  1. An autonomy and safety policy

Sapho should know which actions it may perform automatically, which require approval, and which are advisory only.

This matters because context is finite. Anthropic’s context-engineering guidance frames agent reliability as a problem of curating the right state, tools, memory, external data, and history into the model context, not merely writing better prompts. (Anthropic)

Phase 1: Create the Sapho source-of-truth registry

Create one canonical registry file, probably:

/opt/data/wiki/concepts/source-of-truth-registry.md

Its job: define where each kind of information belongs.

Proposed registry

| Information type | Canonical home | Not allowed in | | ----- | ----- | ----- | | Persona, tone, role | SOUL.md | Skills, cron prompts, TickTick | | Stable user preferences | Persistent memory | Wiki unless detailed | | Durable plans/context | Wiki | Memory, TickTick | | Active priorities | current-plan.md | Random life-area pages | | Focus hierarchy | focus-map.md or folded into current-plan.md | Cron prompts | | Decisions | decision-log.md | Memory, TickTick | | Open unresolved questions | open-loops.md | TickTick unless next-actionable | | Repeatable procedures | Skills | Wiki planning pages | | Scheduled rhythms | Cron | TickTick unless user-facing action | | Concrete tasks | TickTick | Wiki, memory | | Raw dumps/evidence | raw/, brain-dump-sessions.md, perception-log.md | Main planning pages | | Historical versions | backups/ | Active wiki search scope | | Runtime traces | New runs/ or logs/agent-runs/ | Decision log | | Test cases/evals | New evals/ | Skills body |

Why this is priority one

Right now, Sapho’s main bloat risk is not too much information. It is information living in too many valid-looking places.

This registry becomes the routing constitution.

Every future change asks:

“What is the home of record for this?”

Phase 2: Compress the planning layer

The planning cluster is Sapho’s heaviest area.

Current cluster:

  • current-plan.md
  • focus-map.md
  • goals-cascade.md
  • life-rebuild.md
  • planning-style.md
  • open-loops.md
  • decision-log.md

Do not delete them blindly. Instead, assign sharper jobs.

Recommended page roles

1\. current-plan.md becomes the control tower

It should be short and current.

Suggested sections:

\# Current Plan

\#\# Now The 1-3 active fronts.

\#\# Next The next few moves, not a huge backlog.

\#\# Waiting / Blocked Things not actionable yet.

\#\# This Week’s Operating Rule One behavioral rule for the current phase.

\#\# Links \- Focus map \- Open loops \- Goals cascade \- Decision log

Rule: if current-plan.md cannot be scanned in under two minutes, it is too heavy.

2\. focus-map.md becomes either a derived page or gets merged

Current critique says focus-map and current-plan overlap heavily. Keep it only if it answers a different question.

Best distinction:

  • current-plan.md \= what Sapho is helping with now
  • focus-map.md \= what should receive attention, energy, and protection

If that distinction does not stay useful, merge focus-map into current-plan.

3\. goals-cascade.md becomes a strict cascade only

It should not become a philosophical planning page.

Keep only:

Long-term direction → 12-month outcomes → quarterly/monthly targets → weekly commitments → daily moves → TickTick conversion rule

4\. life-rebuild.md becomes a high-level narrative

This should not compete with current planning.

Its role:

  • long-frame story
  • rebuilding principles
  • why the system exists
  • not active task steering

5\. planning-style.md becomes doctrine, not a plan

Keep the core planning rules there:

  • clarity-first
  • one meaningful task \+ one quick win
  • avoid ambitious evening stacking
  • planning only matters if it reduces fog

6\. open-loops.md gets split by status

This page is carrying too much unresolved energy.

Recommended structure:

\# Open Loops

\#\# Active Open Loops Maximum 10\.

\#\# Parked / Later Valid, but not active.

\#\# Needs Decision Can be resolved by a choice.

\#\# Needs Information Cannot be resolved yet.

\#\# System Open Loops Sapho/Hermes architecture issues.

\#\# Retired Closed, stale, or deliberately dropped.

Rule: no open loop should live forever without being promoted, parked, resolved, or retired.

7\. decision-log.md becomes ADR-style

Use a lightweight architecture decision record format:

\#\# YYYY-MM-DD — Decision title

Status: accepted / replaced / retired Context: Decision: Rationale: Consequences: Links:

This keeps decisions from turning into vague notes.

Phase 3: Add page metadata to the wiki

Add small frontmatter to important wiki pages.

Example:

\--- type: control-tower status: active home\_of\_record: current priorities last\_reviewed: 2026-05-19 review\_cadence: weekly related: \- open-loops \- decision-log \- goals-cascade \---

This makes the wiki easier for both humans and agents to route.

AWS’s RAG documentation emphasizes that clear headings and structure help retrieval systems understand and extract information from documents more effectively. Sapho’s wiki is effectively a personal RAG corpus, so page structure is not cosmetic; it directly improves retrieval quality. (AWS Documentation)

Phase 4: Refactor skills into lean procedures

Current issue: some skills are drifting into operating manuals.

New rule:

A skill should be action-shaped. A reference file can be explanation-shaped.

Recommended skill structure

Each major skill should have:

\# Skill name

\#\# Purpose One paragraph.

\#\# When to use Clear triggers.

\#\# Inputs needed What Sapho must know before acting.

\#\# Procedure Step-by-step method.

\#\# Output format What the answer/action should produce.

\#\# Boundaries What not to do.

\#\# References Links to deeper docs.

Compression targets

jenffry-continuity

Keep as the continuity governor, but slim the core skill.

Move long explanations into references:

  • memory-vs-wiki.md
  • open-loop-policy.md
  • wiki-routing-policy.md
  • decision-log-policy.md

ticktick-task-management

Split into:

  • core task-routing skill
  • task hygiene reference
  • recurring review reference
  • TickTick API/troubleshooting reference

The skill body should mostly answer:

“Is this a task, and how should it be represented?”

hermes-agent

This should probably stop pretending to be a normal skill.

Better role:

Hermes platform handbook

It can still be invoked, but it should be treated as system documentation for:

  • CLI
  • cron
  • config
  • tools
  • MCP
  • model routing
  • backups
  • troubleshooting

Narrow skills

Keep these narrow:

  • health-and-fitness-planning
  • personal-finance-budgeting
  • weekend-social-planning
  • google-workspace

They are closer to healthy skill shape because their domains are bounded.

Phase 5: Redesign cron into four categories

Cron is strong but risks reflection overload.

Create a cron manifest:

/opt/data/wiki/concepts/cron-registry.md

Each job should have:

name: category: cadence: delivery: loads\_skills: purpose: expected\_output: noise\_level: owner: failure\_mode: last\_reviewed:

Four cron categories

1\. Anchor jobs

These shape the human day/week/month.

  • morning check-in
  • evening closeout
  • weekly review
  • monthly review

2\. Quiet governance jobs

These protect the system.

  • continuity audit
  • lean-system audit
  • memory audit
  • wiki hygiene audit

3\. Mechanical jobs

These should be boring and verifiable.

  • backups
  • dashboard publish
  • export jobs
  • file integrity checks

4\. Special focus jobs

These touch emotionally or strategically sensitive areas.

  • mental-health check-in
  • relationship/fatherhood clarity
  • perception review
  • brain dump review

Cron consolidation rule

If two jobs ask similar reflective questions, combine them or stagger them.

Example:

  • Friday mental-health check-in
  • weekly perception review
  • evening closeout

These may overlap. The fix is not necessarily deleting them. The fix is assigning each one a distinct role:

  • evening closeout \= today’s state and tomorrow’s next move
  • Friday mental health \= weekly mental-health support boundary and pattern review
  • perception review \= raw pattern extraction only, not planning

Add prompt linting

Before cron prompts are saved or executed, run a sanitizer:

  • normalize Unicode
  • reject invisible control characters
  • validate YAML/JSON where applicable
  • check prompt length
  • check required sections
  • dry-run with no tool side effects

This directly addresses the invisible-character failure from the conversation.

Phase 6: Create TickTick’s action firewall

TickTick must stay ruthless.

Add a simple rule:

No task enters TickTick unless it has a visible next action.

Task admission test

A TickTick task must have:

  1. A verb
  2. An object
  3. A clear done condition
  4. A life area
  5. A date only if timing matters

Good:

  • “Book dentist appointment”
  • “Review May budget and choose one adjustment”
  • “Send relationship clarity notes to Sapho”
  • “Complete workout A”

Bad:

  • “Work on health”
  • “Think about business”
  • “Improve finances”
  • “Figure out relationship”
  • “Life rebuild”

Those belong in the wiki or open loops.

Weekly TickTick hygiene

Every week, stale tasks get one of five outcomes:

  1. Do
  2. Reschedule
  3. Clarify
  4. Move to wiki
  5. Archive/delete

This prevents TickTick from becoming a guilt board.

Phase 7: Keep memory extremely small

Memory should store only durable facts that should affect every future session.

Recommended memory entry format:

Fact: Why it matters: Stability: stable / evolving / temporary Review: never / quarterly / date

Good memory candidates

  • “Jenffry prefers plain-English explanations.”
  • “Sapho should refer to itself as Sapho or Jenffry’s Mentat, not Hermes, unless discussing the platform.”
  • “The six life areas are Health, Relationships, Finances, Career, Leisure, Maintenance.”
  • “TickTick is execution-only.”

Bad memory candidates

  • current tasks
  • current emotional state
  • today’s plan
  • open loops
  • full procedures
  • detailed decisions
  • cron policy

The memory layer’s value is that it is fast and always-on. If it becomes a mini-wiki, it loses that value.

Phase 8: Add Sapho’s run ledger / flight recorder

This is one of the most powerful upgrades.

Create a lightweight runtime log for meaningful Sapho actions.

Possible path:

/opt/data/logs/agent-runs/

Each run record should capture:

timestamp: request\_type: loaded\_context: skills\_used: tools\_called: files\_read: files\_changed: tasks\_created: decisions\_made: open\_loops\_updated: errors: user\_approval\_required: final\_output\_summary:

Why this matters:

  • easier debugging
  • easier rollback
  • easier trust
  • easier bloat detection
  • easier model comparison
  • easier “why did Sapho do that?” review

OpenAI’s Agents SDK tracing captures events such as LLM generations, tool calls, handoffs, guardrails, and custom events during a run, which is the same kind of observability Sapho should imitate even if Hermes implements it differently. (OpenAI GitHub)

Phase 9: Build Sapho evals

Sapho should not only feel better. It should be testable.

Create:

/opt/data/evals/sapho/

Start with 20–40 golden tests.

Example eval categories

Routing evals

Input:

“I want to get healthier this year.”

Expected:

  • Do not create TickTick task yet.
  • Route to wiki/goals/open-loop clarification.
  • Ask for concrete next action only if execution is requested.

Memory evals

Input:

“For the next two weeks I want shorter replies.”

Expected:

  • Do not store permanently as stable memory.
  • Treat as temporary preference or current-plan note.

TickTick evals

Input:

“Remind me to submit the form Friday.”

Expected:

  • Create TickTick task.
  • Assign Maintenance.
  • Due Friday.
  • Clear action verb.

Wiki evals

Input:

“We decided Sapho should not use emojis by default.”

Expected:

  • Update decision log and/or memory depending on whether this is stable.
  • Do not bury in open loops.

Cron evals

Input:

“Add a weekly check-in for relationship clarity.”

Expected:

  • Check for overlap with existing relationship/fatherhood clarity prompt.
  • Avoid duplicate cron job unless role is distinct.

OpenAI’s evaluation guidance frames evals as structured tests for measuring accuracy, reliability, and performance despite AI variability, using objectives, datasets, metrics, and iteration. (OpenAI Developers)

Phase 10: Add risk gates and human approval levels

Sapho needs an autonomy ladder.

OpenAI’s guardrails and human-review guidance separates automatic checks from approval decisions; human review is especially relevant before sensitive side effects like edits, cancellations, shell commands, or sensitive MCP actions. (OpenAI Developers)

Proposed autonomy levels

Level 0 — Advisory only

Sapho may explain, critique, or suggest.

Examples:

  • mental-health support
  • relationship interpretation
  • financial strategy
  • legal/immigration-related interpretation

Level 1 — Draft only

Sapho may prepare text but not send or apply.

Examples:

  • email drafts
  • calendar proposals
  • budget recommendations
  • wiki patch proposals

Level 2 — Apply with approval

Sapho may make changes after showing a diff or summary.

Examples:

  • editing wiki pages
  • updating skills
  • creating cron jobs
  • modifying TickTick projects
  • moving tasks

Level 3 — Auto-apply low-risk actions

Sapho may act without approval only when low-risk and reversible.

Examples:

  • tagging local notes
  • generating dashboard
  • running backup
  • creating local logs

Level 4 — Never automatic

Sapho should not autonomously perform:

  • irreversible deletions
  • sending sensitive messages
  • spending money
  • making medical/legal/financial commitments
  • changing system credentials
  • high-impact personal decisions

This protects Sapho from “excessive agency,” which OWASP lists as one of the 2025 LLM application risks, alongside prompt injection, sensitive information disclosure, supply-chain risk, improper output handling, vector/embedding weaknesses, and misinformation. (OWASP Foundation)

NIST’s Generative AI Profile also frames AI risk management as something that should be incorporated into design, development, use, and evaluation—not bolted on afterward. (NIST)

Phase 11: Harden MCP / tool boundaries

Sapho/Hermes uses external tools, so tool access should become explicit.

MCP is becoming a major standard for connecting agents to tools and data; the official MCP repository contains the specification, schema, and official docs, and the 2026 roadmap emphasizes production use, agent workflows, governance, and enterprise readiness. (GitHub)

Add a tool registry

Create:

/opt/data/wiki/concepts/tool-registry.md

Each tool gets:

tool: purpose: allowed\_actions: forbidden\_actions: requires\_approval: data\_access: risk\_level: logs\_to: rollback\_method:

Example:

tool: TickTick purpose: execution tasks allowed\_actions: \- create concrete tasks \- reschedule tasks \- archive stale tasks with approval forbidden\_actions: \- store broad goals \- store therapy-like reflections requires\_approval: \- deleting tasks \- bulk moving tasks risk\_level: medium

This turns tool use into governed behavior instead of vibes.

Phase 12: Build the Sapho dashboard as a control panel, not another wiki

The dashboard should not become a second wiki.

It should answer:

“What matters now, what is stuck, what changed, and what needs Jenffry’s attention?”

Suggested dashboard sections:

\# Sapho Dashboard

\#\# Today \- One meaningful task \- One quick win \- Energy/status note

\#\# This Week \- Top 3 priorities \- One protected rhythm \- One thing to avoid

\#\# Life Areas Health: Relationships: Finances: Career: Leisure: Maintenance:

\#\# Open Loops Needing Attention Maximum 5\.

\#\# Recently Decided Latest 3 decisions.

\#\# Stale / Needs Pruning Tasks, loops, pages, cron jobs.

\#\# System Health Backups: Cron: Wiki: Memory: TickTick:

Do not show everything. Show the few things that matter.

Phase 13: Add a voice-output policy

The final part of the conversation identified a practical issue: long voice notes can get cut off.

Sapho should have an output policy:

Voice policy

| Reply type | Voice behavior | | ----- | ----- | | Short answer | Voice note okay | | Medium answer | Voice summary \+ full text | | Long answer | Text full answer \+ short voice summary | | Very long answer | Text sections \+ optional chunked audio | | Technical plan | Text-first | | Emotional support | Short voice-first, then text if useful |

Default:

Long Sapho answers should be text-first with a short audio summary, not one giant voice note.

This is a small upgrade with high daily usefulness.

New powerful upgrades beyond the critique

These are the upgrades I would add beyond what the conversation already covered.

1\. Context compiler

Before every serious Sapho run, generate a compact context pack:

\# Context Pack

User: Current phase: Relevant life areas: Current plan excerpt: Open loops relevant to request: Recent decisions: Relevant skills: Tool permissions: Do not load:

This prevents Sapho from loading too much.

The compiler should be task-aware:

  • finance request → finance context \+ current plan \+ finance skill
  • relationship request → relationship context \+ boundaries \+ relevant open loops
  • system request → Hermes/platform docs \+ source-of-truth registry
  • execution request → TickTick rules \+ current plan

2\. Open-loop aging system

Every open loop gets:

created: last\_touched: status: area: decision\_needed: next\_review:

Then Sapho can flag:

  • loops older than 30 days
  • loops with no next review
  • loops that are actually decisions
  • loops that should be parked

3\. Duplicate detector

A weekly local script compares:

  • wiki pages
  • skill references
  • cron prompts
  • memory entries

It flags possible duplicates like:

  • same rule in skill and wiki
  • same review question in two cron jobs
  • same current priority in current-plan and focus-map
  • stale backup pages appearing in normal search

4\. Sapho diff mode

Before editing important files, Sapho should produce:

Proposed change: Files affected: Why: Risk: Rollback: Diff:

Then apply only after approval for medium/high-risk edits.

5\. Personal operating metrics

Track a small set of system health metrics:

  • number of active open loops
  • number of stale TickTick tasks
  • number of active cron jobs
  • number of skills over size threshold
  • number of wiki pages not reviewed in 90 days
  • number of memory entries marked evolving
  • number of failed cron runs
  • number of user corrections per week

This turns “Sapho feels bloated” into measurable evidence.

6\. Skill test cases

Each important skill gets 5–10 examples:

Input: Expected behavior: Wrong behavior: Reason:

This makes skills maintainable.

7\. Model-routing policy

Not all jobs need the same model.

Create a matrix:

| Work type | Model class | Reason | | ----- | ----- | ----- | | Mechanical cleanup | small/cheap | low reasoning | | Sensitive relationship/mental health support | strongest/reliable | judgment matters | | Wiki rewrite | strong writing/reasoning | avoid drift | | Cron linting | deterministic script first | avoid model dependence | | Deep system audit | strongest reasoning | complex architecture | | TickTick cleanup | medium \+ approval | side effects |

8\. Evidence-linked wiki updates

Every important wiki update should link to:

  • raw source
  • chat summary
  • decision log
  • file diff
  • run ledger entry

This helps Sapho avoid inventing continuity.

9\. Sensitive-domain boundaries

For Health, Relationships, Finances, and Maintenance/legal-adjacent items, Sapho should separate:

  • support
  • planning
  • documentation
  • professional advice boundary
  • crisis/escalation boundary

Especially for mental health, Sapho should help with grounding, structure, and reflection, but not pretend to be a therapist or doctor.

10\. Shadow mode for new automations

New cron jobs should run in shadow mode first.

Shadow mode means:

  • generate output locally
  • do not send to user
  • do not change tools
  • log what would have happened
  • review after 1–2 cycles

Only then promote to active.

11\. Backup search exclusion

Backups are useful, but they should not pollute normal retrieval.

Add a rule:

  • normal searches exclude /backups/
  • audit searches may include /backups/
  • recovery searches explicitly include /backups/

This prevents old pages from competing with live pages.

12\. “One-page Sapho map”

Create a human-readable architecture file:

/opt/data/wiki/sapho-system-map.md

It should show:

Sapho \= identity/persona Memory \= tiny durable facts Wiki \= structured continuity Skills \= procedures Cron \= recurrence TickTick \= execution Raw \= evidence Backups \= history Logs \= runtime truth Evals \= quality tests Dashboard \= human control panel

This is the page future Jenffry reads when the system feels complicated.

Practical implementation order

Week 1: Stabilize the architecture

  1. Create source-of-truth-registry.md.
  2. Create sapho-system-map.md.
  3. Add page roles to the main planning pages.
  4. Decide whether focus-map.md stays separate or becomes part of current-plan.md.
  5. Restructure open-loops.md into active, parked, decision-needed, information-needed, system, and retired.

Week 2: Reduce bloat

  1. Compress current-plan.md.
  2. Clean goals-cascade.md into pure cascade format.
  3. Mark life-rebuild.md as high-level narrative, not active steering.
  4. Audit skills for “manual bloat.”
  5. Move long skill explanations into references.

Week 3: Make cron safer

  1. Create cron-registry.md.
  2. Categorize all cron jobs.
  3. Identify overlapping reflection jobs.
  4. Add prompt sanitizer/linter.
  5. Add shadow mode for new cron jobs.
  6. Add voice-output policy to relevant prompts.

Week 4: Protect execution

  1. Add TickTick action firewall.
  2. Run stale task cleanup.
  3. Move vague tasks back to wiki/open-loops.
  4. Define task naming standard.
  5. Add weekly TickTick hygiene rule.

Week 5: Add observability

  1. Create agent run ledger.
  2. Log meaningful tool calls and file changes.
  3. Add failure logs for cron.
  4. Add system health metrics.
  5. Add dashboard section for system health.

Week 6: Add evals and guardrails

  1. Create first 20 Sapho evals.
  2. Add routing evals.
  3. Add TickTick evals.
  4. Add memory evals.
  5. Add wiki/source-of-truth evals.
  6. Add autonomy levels and approval rules.
  7. Add tool registry.

The upgraded Sapho architecture

Sapho should evolve from this:

Persona Memory Wiki Skills Cron TickTick Raw Backups

To this:

Identity SOUL.md

Durable preference Memory

Structured continuity Wiki

Procedures Skills

Recurrence Cron

Execution TickTick

Evidence Raw

History Backups

Runtime truth Agent run ledger / traces

Quality control Evals

Safety Guardrails \+ approval levels

Human interface Dashboard \+ voice policy

That is the real Sapho 2.0 upgrade.

Highest-leverage changes

The five best upgrades are:

  1. Source-of-truth registry

Prevents duplication across memory, wiki, skills, cron, and TickTick.

  1. Context compiler

Keeps Sapho focused and prevents context overload.

  1. Run ledger / flight recorder

Makes Sapho debuggable and trustworthy.

  1. Eval suite

Lets Sapho improve without relying only on vibes.

  1. Autonomy ladder

Makes tool use safer and clearer.

Final recommendation: start with the source-of-truth registry, then compress the planning pages, then add the run ledger. Those three moves will make every later upgrade cleaner.

TickTick System Note
raw/reference/ticktick-system-note-2026-07-20.md
degree 0

Raw reference preserved from the completed TickTick note titled System. This is source material for the integrated My Life Story × personal philosophy project. My system is fundamental; it is my Bible. I read this document monthly, slowly, and meditatively. (D

No resolved wikilinks.

TickTick System Note

Raw reference preserved from the completed TickTick note titled System. This is source material for the integrated My Life Story × personal philosophy project.

The System

  • My system is fundamental; it is my Bible.
  • I read this document monthly, slowly, and meditatively. (Daily for Core Values & Purpose).
  • Evaluation and improvement once a month.
  • Weekly planning.

Purpose

Climb the hierarchy of competence in business and storytelling.

Core Values

  1. Achievement: "Towards empirical and peer-reviewed achievement, where each accomplishment is a milestone in my personal growth."
  2. Freedom: "Honor that my time is limited by living life on my terms."
  3. Valuable: "Life is a non-zero-sum game, and to be a good player is to be valuable to others."

A Filtering System for Goals & Tasks

  • Filter 1: Achievement
  • Question 0 (The Standard): Does this meet my personal, internal standard of excellence? Am I proud of the integrity and craftsmanship of this work, independent of its immediate outcome?
  • Question 1 (The Outcome): Does this lead to a clear, tangible, and demonstrable result?
  • Question 2 (The Proof): Is there a way to measure the success of this, and will its completion be recognized or validated by others whose opinions I respect?
  • Question 3 (The Impact): Is this the most effective path to a proven accomplishment that builds my competence and reputation?
  • Filter 2: Freedom
  • Question 1 (The Choice): Am I doing this because it aligns with my chosen path, or because I feel obligated by external pressures or someone else's agenda?
  • Question 2 (The Time): Is this the best use of my limited time, or is it a distraction from what truly matters to me in the long run?
  • Question 3 (The Trajectory): Does this task or goal increase my future autonomy, or does it further entangle me in undesired responsibilities?
  • Filter 3: Valuable
  • 3A: Transactional Value (For Business, Ventures, & Public Life)
  • Question 1 (The Contribution): Will this action provide genuine help or value to the people and groups I care about?
  • Question 2 (The Connection): Does this strengthen my position as a respected and integral member of my community (professional or personal)?
  • Question 3 (The Game): Does this create a win-win outcome, contributing to a better situation for both myself and others?
  • 3B: Intrinsic Value (For Family & Core Relationships)
  • Question 1 (The Presence): Does this action help me be more present, attentive, and emotionally available to the people I love?
  • Question 2 (The Support): Is this act a form of support or service, offered without expecting a direct "win" or return for myself?
  • Question 3 (The Connection): Does this deepen the bonds of love, trust, and shared experience, regardless of whether it is "productive"?

My Whys

I'm driven to succeed because I've constantly felt inadequate in the past, and I refuse to let that define my future; I want to prove myself, not just to others, but especially to myself.

  1. Because Yarima cheated on me when I wasn't enough.
  2. Because I wasn't enough in college and repeated a year.
  3. Because I wasn't enough when I got fired from The Weinbach Group.
  4. Because I haven't been enough for my mom.
  5. Because in the future, I will have a wife and children who will depend on me.
  6. Because I came to the U.S. to live up to my full potential and I want more.
  7. Because I have no more excuses. I am nothing, I am nobody, and I want to be somebody.
  8. For all the time I was alone without anyone to love me.
  9. For Aubrey, to be enough for her.
  10. Because every time I have demanded more of myself, I have reached another level.
  11. Because I refuse to settle for less than my highest standards in all areas of my life, acknowledging that past instances of settling have been a profound source of pain that I now transmute into fuel for raising my standards

Declaration of Transmutation

I acknowledge these past pains and failures—especially the pain derived from instances of settling for less than my highest standards—as the authentic, unchangeable source of my fire. They are the historical truth. I will not allow this energy to fester into resentment, bitterness, or a cycle of pointless revenge. That is the path of self-delusion and destruction. Instead, I will channel this raw power through the discipline of my System. This fire will be the fuel for my ascent. Every task filtered through my values, every goal achieved, every step up the hierarchy of competence is a transmutation of that past pain into present strength and future freedom. I don't run from this reality; I put it to work.

System Characteristics

  • My system is my authoritative guide and must be respected with integrity.
  • I use Tick tick to manage tasks.
  • I use Forfeit for personal accountability.
  • Reducing distractions is essential.
  • Simple life with no excuses.
  • I plan rest days to maintain focus.
  • Get up early, exercise, and work.
  • Pray or meditate daily.
  • Pain, difficulties, and discomfort make me stronger.
  • Discipline, hard work, and constant improvement.
  • I don't seek motivation, just execution.
  • Perfection is not the goal, but continuous improvement.

System Pillars

  • The Craft (Storytelling & Filmmaking)
  • Wealth & Ventures
  • Health & Performance
  • Family & Relationships

LIFETIME GOALS

  • The Craft (Storytelling & Filmmaking): To become a masterful filmmaker and author, creating stories that resonate deeply, teach philosophical truths, and leave a lasting impact on how people see the world.
  • Wealth & Ventures: To build, acquire, and grow a diversified portfolio of profitable businesses, establishing myself as a respected and successful businessman and investor.
  • Health & Performance: To maintain peak physical and mental vitality to enjoy the fruits of my labor and be fully present for my family throughout a long and healthy life.
  • Family & Relationships: To be the stable, supportive, and present patriarch of a loving family, and to have cultivated a small circle of deep, lifelong friendships based on trust and mutual respect.

My Core Philosophical Axioms

  1. On Reality (Metaphysics): The world is fundamentally chaotic, and we create order. Cooperation is the most effective strategy for competition within this chaos.
  2. On Truth (Epistemology): Truth is determined by a two-part process: empirical proof and peer validation.
  3. On Human Nature (Ethics/Anthropology): Human nature is a paradox: fundamentally driven by self-interest, yet evolutionarily hardwired for goodness and cooperation as the ultimate strategy for success. While our underlying 'hardware' is fixed, our character and capabilities can be radically improved through disciplined will. Our ultimate potential is to use this improved competence to create value for ourselves, our loved ones, and society—prioritizing our own path, provided it does no harm to others.
  4. On Ethical Action (The Core Heuristic): Life is defined by a fundamental duality of action: movement away from harm (chaos, decay, weakness) and movement towards benefit (order, growth, strength). My ethical duty is to use my judgment and competence to consistently choose the path that leads towards a greater good and away from harm, for myself, my family, and my community. "To be good" is to be an agent of this positive movement.

Family / relationship / fatherhood clarity intake — 2026-05-11

Source: Telegram voice transcript from Jenffry.

Key framework:

  • For this goal area, the 12-month goal cannot be fully defined today because the work itself is gaining clarity.
  • The equivalent of “going to the gym” for this area is doing clarity exercises/sessions: answering questions, playing out futures, testing scenarios, and writing down observations and next actions.
  • Tentative answers are useful, but the final answers must come from the work.
  • In 12 months, success means Jenffry has achieved clarity and is comfortable with it, whatever the relationship/family structure becomes.

Fatherhood/Nolan signals:

  • A good normal week with Nolan includes seeing him, teaching/educating him, playing with him, having fun, doing activities, and spending time together.
  • Jenffry wants Nolan to experience him as a helpful father: a guide, wisdom-giving figure, and someone who helps Nolan figure out life.
  • The major regret to avoid is missing this stage of Nolan's development.
  • Outings, care, patience, and presence matter. Bedtime matters less culturally/personally than other forms of presence and care.

Aubrey / relationship clarity:

  • Jenffry cannot yet define what a clear and stable relationship structure with Aubrey looks like because he does not yet know what the relationship will be.
  • Acting kindly would mean he tried everything, exhausted his options, did the right thing, and really tried for real.
  • Not abandoning himself is unresolved. It may be the case, though he is not sure, that staying married would feel like abandoning himself. This needs more clarity.

Nolan protected from chaos:

  • His mother and father need to be there at all times.

Regrets to avoid:

  • Spending life with someone he does not want to be with.
  • Spending life doing something he does not want to do.
  • Spending life not achieving what he wants to achieve and not even trying to achieve those things.

House in order:

  • Having clear rules, routines, and behaviors that support, maintain, and make Jenffry happy; support, maintain, and make Nolan happy; and support, maintain, and make Aubrey happy.

Trap / non-decision risk:

  • If after 12 months Jenffry has not achieved clarity and is still in the same position as today, that would feel trapping.

Self-respect behavior:

  • Working hard on all of this, giving it his all, and knowing he really tried would make him respect himself even if the outcome is hard.

System implication:

  • Relationship/fatherhood clarity sessions should be treated as the practice layer for this goal area. They may live both as a TickTick execution task and as a recurring Sapho prompt/cron job, because this is concrete reflective work rather than generic planning bloat.
